content.text:
## 1. Jurisdictional limits — MAXIMUM AMOUNT YOU CAN SUE FOR

**Dollar cap:** Louisiana small claims courts can award a maximum of **$5,000** per claim (excluding interest and court costs) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=A,the%20Code%20of%20Civil%20Procedure)). This cap applies across the board; **no higher limit for individuals vs. businesses** – all plaintiffs are held to $5,000. The $5,000 limit is *per case*, not per defendant or per plaintiff, and it does **not** include any interest, attorney fees, or statutory penalties you might also recover ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=A,the%20Code%20of%20Civil%20Procedure)).

**Multiple plaintiffs/claims:** You cannot exceed $5,000 by joining claims or plaintiffs. If multiple people sue together, the total combined award still cannot top $5,000. Louisiana law actually forbids more than **10 plaintiffs** joining in one small claim action ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=not%20exceed%20five%20thousand%20dollars%2C,of%20the%20Code%20of%20Civil)) (to prevent end-runs around the cap). **Splitting one claim into multiple suits** is also not allowed – you can’t sue someone twice over parts of the same debt or injury just to fit under the limit. If you do, the court can dismiss the later suit as an improper “splitting” of a cause of action (res judicata may bar the second claim).

**Plaintiff type differences:** Louisiana does **not** set different small-claims limits by plaintiff type (unlike some states). **Individuals, corporations, LLCs, partnerships, and sole proprietors** all face the same $5,000 cap in small claims. Government entities generally do not use small claims court as plaintiffs, and if you need to sue a *government entity* (city, state agency, etc.), small claims court is usually not the forum (government bodies have immunity and specialized claim procedures – see “What you CANNOT sue for”). 

**Frequent filer limits:** While Louisiana doesn’t impose a statewide numeric limit on how many small claims a person or business can file per year, **courts may set “mass filing” limits by local rule** ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=D,in%20the%20small%20claims%20divisions)). This is intended to curb collection agencies or others from flooding small claims court. For example, a city court could cap the number of filings per month by the same plaintiff. Always check the local small claims court rules if you plan to file **many claims** (e.g. a business with frequent customer debts). 

**Exceeding the cap:** If your claim is worth more than $5,000, you have two choices: (1) **forego any amount above $5,000** and sue in small claims for $5,000 max, or (2) file in a higher court (regular City/Parish/District Court) to pursue the full amount. You *cannot* recover more than $5,000 in a Louisiana small claim case – any judgment will be reduced to the cap. If a **counterclaim** over $5,000 arises, the case **must be transferred** out of small claims: Louisiana law lets the defendant file a separate suit in a court of competent jurisdiction and then have the small claim consolidated into it ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=1238489#:~:text=B,commenced%20the%20small%20claims%20action)) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=1238489#:~:text=D,such%20other%20docket%20or%20court)). (In practice, a defendant with a claim above $5,000 can file a motion to “remove” or transfer the case to the regular docket, as discussed in the Counterclaims section below.)

**No class actions:** Louisiana explicitly bans class-action lawsuits in small claims court ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=more%20than%20ten%20parties%20plaintiff,of%20the%20Code%20of%20Civil)). You must file individually – you **cannot** represent a class of similarly affected people.

**Equitable relief limits:** Small claims courts here *can* award equitable relief (like ordering someone to return property or cancel a contract) if it’s appropriate ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=B,executory%20proceedings%20shall%20be%20prohibited)). But **injunctions and restraining orders** (court orders commanding someone to do or stop doing something under threat of contempt) are *not* available in small claims ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=B,executory%20proceedings%20shall%20be%20prohibited)). In other words, a small claims judge can order someone to pay money or even undo a deal, but **cannot issue an injunction** except to enforce its own judgment. The relief generally must be monetary or closely tied to the $5,000 dispute.

**Special venue exceptions:** The $5,000 limit applies in all City Court small-claim divisions statewide. One quirk – in the City Court of East St. Tammany, the small claims division’s limit is defined by the Justice of the Peace court limit ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=E,Procedure%20Articles%20591%20through%20597)). Since Justice of the Peace (JP) courts in Louisiana also have a $5,000 civil jurisdiction ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=112118#:~:text=A,not%20exceed%20five%20thousand%20dollars)), this is effectively the same $5,000 cap. (JP courts are discussed below; they handle small claims in rural areas without city courts.) Eviction cases are an exception to the monetary cap in some courts (see the Landlord–Tenant section and county-level differences).

**Bottom line:** $5,000 is the maximum you can sue for in Louisiana small claims court. If your claim is even one dollar over, you’ll need to waive the excess or choose a different court. Louisiana does not allow any tricks to expand this limit – no multiple filings on a single dispute, no splitting of claims, and no joining lots of plaintiffs to multiply the award. The cap has been **$5,000 since 2020** (previously $3,000), so make sure your claim (not counting interest/costs) meets this threshold.

## 2. What types of cases qualify — EVERYTHING YOU CAN SUE FOR

Louisiana small claims court can handle a **wide array of civil disputes** as long as the remedy sought is money (or in some cases, return of property) not exceeding $5,000. The court’s mandate is to do “substantial justice” between the parties with relaxed procedure ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=A,is%20founded%20upon%20competent%20evidence)). Below is an **exhaustive list** of case types commonly brought, with notes on Louisiana-specific rules, examples, and any special requirements or limits. If your situation fits one of these, it’s likely eligible for small claims – if not, we’ll flag it in the later “cannot sue” section.

### Contract & money-owed disputes

- **Breach of written contract:** If someone fails to perform a written agreement – e.g. a contractor doesn’t finish the job you had a contract for, or a client refuses to pay for services – you can sue for the financial loss (up to $5,000). *Examples:* A caterer contracted in writing to service an event but never showed – you could sue for the deposit and any extra costs incurred. Or if you paid a contractor $4,000 under a written home repair contract and they didn’t do the work, you can sue to get that money back. **Louisiana quirk:** Written contracts generally have a long prescriptive period (10 years to sue on a breach) ([www.nolo.com](https://www.nolo.com/legal-encyclopedia/louisiana-small-claims-court-31722.html#:~:text=How%20Much%20Time%20Do%20I,Small%20Claims%20Court%20in%20Louisiana)), so even older breaches may still be actionable in small claims. There’s no special pre-suit requirement; however, it’s wise to send a **demand letter** (not required by law for basic breach, but good practice). If the contract has an attorney-fee clause or other remedies, you can claim those in small claims (though remember the $5,000 limit). Louisiana’s evidence rules are relaxed in small claims ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=A,is%20founded%20upon%20competent%20evidence)), so even if the contract required certain notices or documents, the judge’s main concern is that a valid agreement existed and was broken.

- **Breach of oral/handshake agreement:** Oral contracts are binding in Louisiana (except a few that *must* be written, like real estate sales). You can sue on a purely verbal promise if you can convince the judge it existed and was breached. *Examples:* You paid someone $500 on a verbal promise they’d paint your fence, but they never did. Or you agreed to trade a used appliance plus $200 for some work on your car, you paid but they didn’t do their part. These are valid small claims. Louisiana doesn’t require any writing for small claims suits – you’ll simply state under oath what was agreed. Tip: Bring any **texts, emails, or witnesses** that corroborate the oral deal. *Statute of limitations:* Oral contracts are generally classified as “personal actions” (10-year period) unless they fall under “open accounts” or other categories (see Loans/Open accounts below). So you typically have up to 10 years to sue on a purely oral contract ([www.nolo.com](https://www.nolo.com/legal-encyclopedia/louisiana-small-claims-court-31722.html#:~:text=How%20Much%20Time%20Do%20I,Small%20Claims%20Court%20in%20Louisiana)) (practically, evidence might get harder to obtain with time). No formal pre-suit notice is required, but again a polite demand can help resolve it or serve as evidence.

- **Unpaid personal loans (friends & family IOUs):** If you lent someone money informally and haven’t been paid back, that’s a classic small claim. *Examples:* You lent a friend $2,000 with a verbal promise of repayment in a year; it’s now two years and nothing. Or you covered your cousin’s $500 rent one month on a promise to be repaid, and they ghosted you. Louisiana treats a personal loan as either a contract or “money lent” under the law. **State quirk:** “Money lent” is one of the obligations with a **3-year prescriptive period** ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=fails%20or%20refuses%20to%20comply,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)) (meaning you should sue within 3 years of when it was due to be repaid). So don’t wait too long. No special steps needed before suing – though providing some written demand or accounting is courteous. Small claims judges often see these cases; bring any evidence of the loan (emails, bank transfers, a note if one exists, or testimony under oath about the agreement).

- **Unpaid promissory notes:** A promissory note is a written promise to pay (like an IOU with specific terms). Suing on a note is straightforward: you gave money or goods, they signed a note to repay by X date, and they failed to pay. *Example:* You sold someone a used boat for $6,000 with $1,000 down and a note for $5,000; only $2,000 has been paid and $3,000 remains – you can sue for the $3,000 balance (within the cap). Louisiana law gives **5 years** to sue on promissory notes and other negotiable instruments ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=110524#:~:text=Art,to%20run%20from%20the%20day)). If the note has an attorney-fee clause, you can ask for those fees (subject to the $5k cap). No special pre-suit notice is required (the note itself usually states when due). Be sure to attach a **copy of the note** to your claim or bring the original to court; under Louisiana evidence rules, the judge may want to see the original note (especially if it has an endorsement on it). Default judgments on notes are easier since the amount is clear – Louisiana procedure even allows obtaining a default **without a hearing** if it’s a sum certain on a note ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=112113#:~:text=C,minute%20clerk%20shall%20make%20an)) (the judge can sign the default judgment based on the submitted note and affidavit).

- **Unpaid commercial invoices / accounts receivable:** If you’re a business (or individual) owed money on an **open account** – e.g. a customer hasn’t paid their invoice for goods or services – you can use small claims as long as the balance is $5,000 or less. Louisiana’s **Open Account Statute** provides an extra benefit: if the customer didn’t pay within 30 days of a written demand, you can recover **reasonable attorney’s fees** along with the balance ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/law.aspx?d=107205#:~:text=A,on%20the%20claim%20is%20rendered)). *Examples:* A freelance graphic designer issued a $1,200 invoice that’s 90 days overdue; a supplier sold $4,000 in parts to a mechanic on account, and the mechanic never paid. These fit small claims. **Pre-suit requirement:** To get attorney fees under La. R.S. 9:2781, you must send a **written demand** correctly stating the amount owed and wait 30 days ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/law.aspx?d=107205#:~:text=A,on%20the%20claim%20is%20rendered)). Include a copy of the unpaid invoice(s). Even if you’re not hiring an attorney, you can still claim those attorney-fee damages (some judges might award a pro-rata amount or a nominal fee for your time). Be prepared to prove the delivery of goods/services and the unpaid balance. Also, be aware open-account claims prescribe in **3 years** in Louisiana ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)).

- **Unpaid rent (residential and commercial):** A landlord can sue a former tenant (or current tenant who’s moved out mid-lease) for **unpaid rent** up to $5,000. *Example:* A tenant broke the lease and left owing 2 months’ rent ($1,800) – you can sue for that rent and possibly late fees. You cannot use small claims to evict a tenant (evictions are separate; see Landlord–Tenant below), but you can sue solely for rent owed or damages after the tenant is out. Louisiana treats rent as an “open account” type of obligation: **past-due rent has a 3-year prescriptive period** ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)). If there’s a written lease, attach it; if oral (which is legal for month-to-month), provide testimony or receipts showing the rent terms. No special demand is required by statute to sue for rent, but a **notice to vacate or pay** might have been given if eviction was attempted – bring any such notices to show the context. For residential leases, also see the **security deposit** subsection (landlords must follow deposit return law). For unpaid rent, you can also claim any legitimate late fees or NSF check charges per the lease (so long as the total is within $5k). If the tenant’s check bounced, also see “bounced checks” below for additional damages.

- **Bounced/dishonored checks:** Louisiana provides a strong remedy for NSF (non-sufficient funds) checks. If someone paid you with a check that **bounced**, you can sue not only for the check amount but also statutory **penalties**. Specifically, if you send a written demand (by certified mail) and the check writer doesn’t pay within 15 working days, they are liable for **double the check amount (or $100, whichever is more), plus court costs and reasonable attorney fees** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=A,attorney%20fees%20and%20court%20costs)). This is authorized by La. R.S. 9:2782. *Example:* A customer’s $500 check to you bounced. You sent the required certified letter and got no response. In small claims you can sue for $500 + $500 penalty + atty fees and costs ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=A,attorney%20fees%20and%20court%20costs)). The total penalty is capped at twice the check (minimum $100), so even if the double amount exceeds $5,000, **the $5,000 court limit still applies** – e.g. a $3,000 bad check could statutorily incur $6,000 in double damages, but you’d be limited to $5,000 here and might want to file in a higher court to get the full penalty. **Procedure:** You *must* send a **certified demand letter** to the check’s maker, giving them 15 working days to pay the amount plus a service charge (up to $25 or 5% of the check) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=pay%20the%20obligation%20created%20by,attorney%20fees%20and%20court%20costs)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=C,address%20shown%20on%20the%20instrument)). Use the statutory wording in R.S. 9:2782(C) for the letter ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=C,address%20shown%20on%20the%20instrument)). If they still don’t pay, bring proof of mailing the letter (and whether it was unclaimed or refused – it still counts as long as sent to the address on the check ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=,the%20person%20making%20the%20check))). Include a copy of the bounced check and bank NSF notice. Louisiana also criminalizes writing bad checks, but your small claim is a **civil action for damages** – you can pursue it regardless of any criminal complaint. Many defendants will pay up when they learn of the double-damages law.

- **Open book accounts / running tabs:** If you have an ongoing **open account** with someone – for example, a running tab at a store, a charge account with a service provider, or any scenario where a balance carried over with multiple transactions – you can sue for the unpaid balance (again up to $5k). This is essentially the same as the “accounts receivable” discussed above. *Examples:* A farm feed supplier had an open account for a customer who bought feed monthly; the customer stopped paying with $2,500 outstanding. Or a bar allowed a regular to run a tab that ballooned to $600. Provided you can show the balance and that the account was kept in the regular course of business, it qualifies as an open account. Louisiana’s open account attorney-fee law (30-day demand) applies here as well ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/law.aspx?d=107205#:~:text=A,on%20the%20claim%20is%20rendered)). Be mindful of the **3-year limit** on suing for such debts ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)), counted from the date of the last charge or last payment on the account (Louisiana courts consider partial payments as restarting the clock for prescription in many cases). Bring a **statement of account** or ledger showing how the balance was calculated, and any terms that were agreed on (interest on past-due amounts, etc., though interest might push the total over $5k). You can claim interest up to the date of judgment as long as the principal + interest doesn’t exceed $5,000 in the claim (alternatively, waive some interest to keep it in small claims).

- **Co-signer / guarantor disputes:** If you co-signed a loan or guaranteed someone’s payment and then *you* had to pay because they defaulted, you can sue the primary debtor in small claims for what you paid on their behalf. *Example:* You co-signed your friend’s $4,000 car loan; they defaulted and you ended up paying the bank $3,000. You can sue your friend for indemnification of that $3,000. This is essentially an equitable obligation – Louisiana law allows a surety (co-signer) who pays a debt to step into the shoes of the creditor and collect from the principal debtor. Provide evidence of the guarantee (e.g. a copy of the loan note you co-signed) and proof you paid (receipts or bank statements). The prescriptive period might be viewed as the same period as the original debt or possibly 10 years (written contract subrogation). Either way, it’s likely timely if within a few years. Co-signer suits are appropriate for small claims as long as the amount is within the cap. No special rules – treat it like any other breach of contract or debt claim.

### Consumer disputes

- **Refunds for defective products:** If you bought a product that turned out to be defective or not working as promised, and the seller refuses to refund or repair, you can sue for a refund or damages up to $5k. Louisiana’s law of “redhibition” protects buyers of defective products. *Examples:* You bought a used appliance for $800 that died after a week and the seller won’t refund; or a new computer part ($200) that was dead on arrival and the store won’t replace it. In small claims, you typically sue for the **purchase price** and maybe any repair costs or consequential damages (like costs to diagnose the defect) as long as the total is under $5k. **Louisiana quirk – Redhibition:** In Louisiana, if a product has a **redhibitory defect** (a defect that would have prevented you from buying it had you known), you’re entitled to return the item for a refund (or a reduction in price if you keep it) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=109585#:~:text=A,the%20buyer%2C%20whichever%20occurs%20first)). If the seller knew of the defect, you can also get attorney fees ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=or%20deceptive%20method%2C%20act%2C%20or,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)). In small claims, you can indeed ask the judge to rescind the sale (an equitable remedy) or award a partial refund – small claims divisions *can* grant equitable relief like cancellation of a sale ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=B,executory%20proceedings%20shall%20be%20prohibited)). *Pre-suit:* It’s wise (though not legally required in all cases) to notify the seller and give them a chance to fix or refund. If you do that in writing, it helps show you acted reasonably. For new consumer goods, there may also be manufacturer warranties; you can still use small claims to enforce a warranty if the manufacturer or seller isn’t honoring it. Louisiana’s redhibition prescriptive period is **1 year from discovery of the defect**, up to a max of 4 years from purchase if the seller was in good faith ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=109585#:~:text=A,the%20buyer%2C%20whichever%20occurs%20first)) (and for sellers who knew of the defect, 1 year from discovery or up to 10 years from sale ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=109585#:~:text=B,of%20sale%2C%20whichever%20occurs%20first))). So don’t delay once you know the problem. Bring the defective product (if portable) or photos/videos demonstrating the issue, and any expert report or repair estimate if you have one (not required, but helpful for technical defects).

- **Refunds for services not performed or done poorly:** If you paid for a service and the provider either failed to perform or did a shoddy job, you can sue to get your money back (or the cost to fix their work). *Examples:* You paid a lawncare company $300 for a season, and they never showed up; or you hired a photographer for an event, paid a $500 deposit, and they disappeared; or a home painter did paint your house but in such a substandard way that you had to hire someone else to redo it for $1,000 – you can claim that $1,000. In Louisiana, service contracts are generally treated like any other contract – you’re entitled to the benefit of the bargain. If the service was not performed at all, you seek a refund. If performed poorly, you typically seek the cost of corrective measures (or difference in value). *Tip:* Document the deficiencies (photos of the poor workmanship, correspondence where you complained, etc.). No special rule requires a pre-suit demand, but it’s highly recommended to ask for a refund or re-performance first – especially if you might later claim the provider *knew* of problems (could support a claim under Louisiana’s unfair trade practices law if egregious). **UDAP note:** If the poor service was accompanied by deceptive or unfair acts (for example, a repair shop knowingly overcharged or lied about having done work), you might invoke Louisiana’s Unfair Trade Practices Act (LUTPA) in your claim. LUTPA allows recovery of actual damages and a possible **treble damages** if the conduct was knowingly wrongful after notice from authorities ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=representative%20capacity%20to%20recover%20actual,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)), plus attorney fees ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=notice%20by%20the%20attorney%20general%2C,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)). However, LUTPA claims are complex and treble damages require prior notice by the Attorney General, so most small claims will just be straightforward breach of contract or negligence claims. Prescriptive period: service contract breaches generally fall under 10-year (if written) or possibly 3-year if viewed as compensation for services (wages/fees) under Civil Code 3494. If in doubt, assume possibly 3 years from when the service was done or supposed to be done, and file timely.

- **Auto repair overcharges or shoddy work:** You can use small claims to address disputes with mechanics and body shops. *Examples:* A mechanic charged you $800 for repairs that were never actually done (or did unnecessary work), or they did a repair but the car still isn’t fixed and their work was faulty, causing you damage. In Louisiana, auto repair shops are regulated by general contract law and some specific statutes (e.g. they must be licensed, provide estimates in some cases). You can sue for the difference between what you paid and the value of what you got. If you had to pay another mechanic to fix the first mechanic’s mistakes, you can claim that cost. Evidence is key: get a second mechanic’s written statement explaining what was done wrong or not done. There’s no special required notice, but writing a complaint to the shop giving them a chance to cure can bolster your case (and you might mention you’ll take it to court or to the Attorney General’s Consumer Protection). If the shop engaged in deceptive practice (e.g. charging for parts not installed), that could fall under **fraud** or LUTPA as well – you could mention La. R.S. 51:1405 (unfair trade practices) and request attorney fees under LUTPA ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=representative%20capacity%20to%20recover%20actual,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)). Keep the claim simple in small claims though; focus on the dollar loss. You generally have the standard prescriptive period – likely 10 years if you have a written invoice/contract, or possibly 1 year if you frame it as tort (negligent repair that damaged your car). It’s safest to file within 1 year of discovering the bad work to avoid any debate.

- **Home repair / contractor disputes (incomplete work, defects, abandonment):** Home improvement contractors who take your money and run, or do substandard work, are prime targets for small claims (provided the disputed amount is ≤ $5k). *Examples:* You paid a handyman $2,500 to remodel a bathroom; he did half the work and vanished. Or a roofer fixed your roof for $4,000 but it still leaks because the work was defective. You can sue for the unfinished work’s value or the cost to repair defects. If the contractor was required to be licensed (in Louisiana, jobs over a certain amount require licensing), mention that if relevant, though licensing issues don’t preclude your recovery – they actually strengthen your case if the contractor violated regulations. **Pre-suit notice:** Not legally mandatory in most cases, but consider sending a **written punch-list or demand** to the contractor to either fix the issues or refund a portion, with a deadline, before suing. This can show the judge you gave them a fair chance. If a contractor abandoned the job after taking advance payment, you might also consider filing a complaint with the Louisiana State Licensing Board for Contractors (if licensed) – but that’s separate from getting your money back, which small claims can do. Louisiana also has criminal statutes for contractor fraud (for example, taking money and not performing within 45 days with intent to defraud), but again, your small claim is a civil route. Bring evidence: the contract (if written), photos of incomplete/defective work, receipts for any additional costs to fix, and any texts/emails where the contractor made excuses or admissions. The prescriptive period for construction contracts is typically 10 years (if written), but defective construction might also be framed as a tort (1 year from damage). If a building defect caused property damage (e.g. shoddy plumbing work flooded your floor), that property damage portion is subject to 1-year delictual prescription. So, it’s safest to file within 1 year of the issue.

- **Appliance/electronics warranty failures:** If a product you bought is under warranty and the manufacturer or seller isn’t honoring the warranty, you can sue for the cost of repair or replacement (or a refund if appropriate). *Example:* You bought a refurbished TV with a 6-month warranty, it died in 3 months, and the seller refuses to fix or replace it – you could sue for the cost of a replacement TV or your money back. Another example: a used car came with a limited 30-day warranty, it broke down in 2 weeks and the dealer won’t repair – you can sue (if within $5k) for the repair cost or to undo the sale in some cases. Louisiana’s redhibition law might come into play for used items (see defective products above) – a used item sold “as is” limits your redhibition rights unless the seller committed fraud. But if there *was* a warranty, you have a contractual right to enforcement. No special pre-suit steps beyond trying to use the warranty’s official process (e.g. contacting the manufacturer’s service center). Document those attempts – it shows the court you followed procedure but were ignored. Small claims can grant rescission (cancellation) or price reduction in appropriate cases ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=B,executory%20proceedings%20shall%20be%20prohibited)), which is essentially what enforcing a warranty might involve. Ensure to file within any warranty period or the general prescriptive period for contract (10 years, or 1 year in redhibition for latent defects). If suing a manufacturer not based in Louisiana, you’ll need to ensure they can be served (if they do business in the state, you can use Louisiana’s long-arm statute; if not, it may be tricky – see “service of process” section).

- **Cancelled or no-show service providers (photographers, caterers, event venues, etc.):** If you hired someone for an event or service on a specific date (wedding vendor, DJ, caterer, etc.) and they canceled last-minute or simply didn’t show up, causing you loss, you can sue for what you paid and any additional costs you incurred. *Example:* You put down a $1,000 deposit for a wedding photographer who never came – you can sue for the $1,000 plus perhaps any extra money you spent to get a replacement on short notice (if under the cap). These cases are straightforward breaches if you had a contract. Even if the contract had a “no refund” clause, if they provided nothing, generally that clause wouldn’t hold up (they can’t keep payment without performing). Louisiana allows recovery of out-of-pocket losses and possibly foreseeable additional expenses due to the breach. Keep receipts of emergency replacements or any damages. Sometimes event contracts have arbitration clauses or liquidated damages clauses; check if yours does – but small claims won’t handle arbitrations, you’d have to go to arbitration if binding. If the vendor gave you short notice of cancellation and you had to pay more for a replacement, you might also frame it as an **unfair trade practice** if there was deceit involved (though likely it’s just breach). No special notice needed; just sue and serve them (if an out-of-state vendor, ensure jurisdiction). The prescriptive period is 10 years for written contracts; 10 or 3 for oral (depending on circumstances).

- **Dry cleaning / property loss in custody (e.g. at cleaners, valet, repair shop):** If a business lost or damaged your property that you entrusted to them, you can sue for the value (or cost of repair). *Examples:* A dry cleaner lost your expensive coat; a valet service returned your car with damage; a phone repair shop broke your device or lost components. These often come down to negligence or breach of a bailment contract. In small claims, you can absolutely pursue these. Bring proof of the item’s value (receipts, photos, market value). Many dry cleaners or similar services limit their liability via fine print (e.g. “not responsible for items over $100 value” or “will pay 10x the cleaning cost”). Louisiana courts may enforce reasonable limitations in contracts, but if the limitation is unconscionable or not communicated, you still can argue for full value. Since small claims judges seek equitable results ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=B,provisions%20relating%20to%20privileged%20communications)), they may not strictly enforce a tiny cap if an expensive item was lost and the customer wasn’t clearly informed. *Pre-suit:* It’s good to give the business a chance to pay or resolve (most reputable places have claims processes). If they deny your claim or offer an inadequate amount, that’s when you file. Legally, damage or loss to property in their custody can be a tort (negligence) – prescriptive period **1 year from when you knew of the loss/damage**. So don’t delay. If it’s purely contractual (e.g. a paid service), one could argue longer, but safer to treat as 1 year. As evidence, present any claim tickets, contracts, or signs showing terms, as well as your testimony about what happened and the item’s worth.

- **Gym/subscription/membership cancellation refunds:** Louisiana consumers often struggle to get refunds or stop charges for gym memberships, online subscriptions, cable/Internet services, etc. If you properly canceled a membership or service and the company kept charging you (or failed to refund a prepaid amount), you can sue for those amounts. *Example:* You paid a gym $600 upfront for a year, but you moved after 3 months and canceled per the contract – the gym never refunded the unused portion. Or you canceled a monthly subscription but continue to get billed $50 a month. Small claims is a good venue for these relatively small sums. Louisiana has a law on health club contracts (requiring certain cancellation rights in some cases), but even aside from that, it’s a straightforward breach or unjust enrichment claim. *Pre-suit steps:* Document your cancellation – copies of the cancellation letter or email, proof of mailing, or notes of the cancellation confirmation number or person you spoke with. Many big companies will issue a refund once served with a suit (they often don’t even appear, leading to a default judgment). If it’s an **automatic renewal** situation (like a magazine or a service that auto-renewed without clear consent), Louisiana has an Auto-Renewal statute (La. R.S. 9:2716) requiring clear disclosure of auto-renew clauses. If they violated that, mention it; it might entitle you to cancel and get a refund. But even without citing laws, just clearly show you canceled and they kept your money. There’s no special limitation beyond normal contract limits (10 years written). However, if recurring charges, don’t wait too long – you typically can’t claim beyond what occurred in the last few years due to concepts like course of dealing (and practically, older than 3 years might face a defense if seen as periodic payments). Suing in small claims often prompts quick settlement from companies due to the hassle.

- **Auto dealer/used-car disputes (small-scale “lemon” issues, odometer fraud, undisclosed defects):** Major lemon law cases exceed $5k (usually you’d sue in regular court for a new car refund), but there are scenarios where a used-car buyer or any car buyer might sue in small claims. *Examples:* You bought a used car for $4,500 “as is” but later discovered the dealer rolled back the odometer or failed to disclose a salvage title – you could sue for the diminished value or rescission up to $5k. Or a dealer promised to fix a certain issue as part of the sale and didn’t, and the cost to fix is under $5k. Louisiana’s **Lemon Law** (New Motor Vehicle Warranty Act, R.S. 51:1941+) applies to new vehicles and gives rights to a replacement or refund for serious defects, but typically those claims exceed the small claims limit (the vehicle price). However, if your claim under the Lemon Law is essentially for incidental costs or a minor issue under $5k, you could attempt small claims (though generally lemon law cases go through a specified arbitration process first, so small claims might not be the proper route for formal lemon law relief). For **used cars**, Louisiana relies on redhibition and fraud laws. If a used car dealer committed **odometer fraud**, federal law (odometer act) allows a civil suit for treble damages or $10,000, but that’s above small claims – you could waive the excess and sue for $5k as a simplified approach (or just sue under state law for the $5k difference in value). If a defect was intentionally hidden, you could get attorney fees in redhibition ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=or%20deceptive%20method%2C%20act%2C%20or,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)), though again you’re limited by the small claims cap. *Note:* Louisiana’s Redhibition prescriptive period for used car defects often will be 1 year from sale or discovery, so act promptly ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=109585#:~:text=A,the%20buyer%2C%20whichever%20occurs%20first)). You should also notify the seller as soon as you discover a serious defect and allow them an opportunity to repair (this is actually a requirement to pursue redhibition against a good-faith seller – you must give them notice to repair). Document that notice (certified letter describing the problem). If they refuse or it fails, you can sue for the cost of repair or cancellation of sale (if within $5k). A common small claims scenario: **undisclosed salvage or flood vehicle** – you might sue for the difference between what you paid and the true value or for rescission if the price was < $5k. Another: **missing title** or paperwork, where dealer fails to deliver title – you could sue for a refund if you can’t use the car.

- **False advertising / deceptive trade practices:** If you were induced into a purchase by false advertising or a scammy business practice that cost you money, you can sue the business in small claims for your losses. Louisiana’s Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law (LUTPA, La. R.S. 51:1401 et seq.) **allows private suits** ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=A,such%20action%20reasonable%20attorney%20fees)). *Examples:* A store advertised a television at $300 but charged you $500 with add-ons you didn’t agree to, or a company misrepresented what a product could do, and you spent $200 on it uselessly. In small claims, you generally seek **actual damages** – the money you lost. LUTPA provides that if the conduct was willful and the Attorney General had notified the business that it was unlawful, the court *shall* award treble (triple) damages ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=representative%20capacity%20to%20recover%20actual,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)). In practice, that condition (AG notice) is rarely met in a typical case. But you can still mention LUTPA and request attorney’s fees, because the law says the court *shall award attorney fees* to a prevailing consumer ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=notice%20by%20the%20attorney%20general%2C,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)). In small claims, since you likely don’t have an attorney, this might be moot, but you could argue for some compensation for your time under that statute. *Pre-suit:* There’s no requirement to complain to the Attorney General first, but you can file a complaint with the Louisiana AG’s Consumer Protection Section, which sometimes helps mediate. However, doing so is optional. The prescriptive period under LUTPA is **1 year from the violation** (it’s considered one-year, per La. R.S. 51:1409(E), a peremptive period actually). So you must sue within 1 year of the deceptive act. Gather evidence of the advertising (screenshots, brochures) and how it was false. Note: if your damages are small, a judge might find no LUTPA violation if it seems like a minor contract breach rather than egregious fraud. But small claims judges can still apply LUTPA if clearly breached. 

### Property damage

- **Vehicle collisions (where damages are within the cap):** If you were in a car accident and the damage to your vehicle (and any minor out-of-pocket costs) is $5,000 or less, you can sue the at-fault driver in small claims. *Example:* Minor fender-bender caused $3,200 in repair bills and $400 car rental – $3,600 total, well within the limit. Personal injury claims from car accidents are usually above $5k (see “personal injury” below), but property damage alone often is within small claims range for minor accidents. **Louisiana specifics:** Louisiana is a **fault state** for auto accidents – the at-fault party (or their insurance) owes the damages. You can sue the driver, and in Louisiana you can also name their insurer directly in many cases (Louisiana’s “direct action” statute allows suing the liability insurer if the accident occurred in Louisiana or the policy was written here) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=508142#:~:text=Louisiana%20Laws%20,or%20delivered%20in%20this%20state)). It can be strategic to name the insurer to ensure any judgment is paid. However, in small claims, often only the individual will appear (insurance companies *can* send an attorney, but in small claims they might not, or they might try to remove to a higher court if the insurer is named – note that adding an insurer might prompt the case being moved out of small claims if the insurer wants a more formal forum, since small claims allows attorneys and removal anyway). To keep it simple, you might just sue the other driver, and they will likely turn it over to their insurer who may settle or defend. *Evidence:* Bring repair estimates or bills, photos of the damage, and proof the other driver was at fault (police report, witness statement). If you already received partial insurance payouts (like if you had coverage and your insurer paid minus deductible), clarify what portion you’re claiming (e.g. your $500 deductible that wasn’t covered, or other losses like rental not fully covered). **Prescriptive period:** Property damage from a vehicle accident is a **delictual action**, prescribing in **1 year** from the accident (or from when damage was sustained) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/2011/cc/cc3492?mf_ct_campaign=aol-synd-feed#:~:text=2011%20Louisiana%20Laws%20Civil%20Code,It)). So you must file within one year. Also be aware of Louisiana’s “No Pay, No Play” law – if you, as a plaintiff, were an uninsured driver, you cannot recover the first $15,000 of personal injuries and the first $25,000 of property damage from others. In small claims property cases, if you were uninsured, essentially you can’t recover at all if damage < $25k (the law would bar it). That’s a complex rule; hopefully as a plaintiff you had insurance or it doesn’t apply (it doesn’t apply to parked car or clear non-driving situations). In small claims, a judge might or might not enforce that strictly if not raised, but legally it’s a bar if you were uninsured. If both parties were insured or you were not driving, no issue.

- **Damage to personal property by another person:** Any situation where someone negligently or intentionally damages your belongings can be brought as a small claim if the repair or replacement cost is within $5k. *Examples:* A guest at your house accidentally knocks over and breaks your $600 TV and refuses to pay; a neighbor’s kid threw a baseball through your window ($200 damage); your friend borrowed an item and returned it broken. These are generally **tort claims** for property damage (one-year prescription from the incident) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/2011/cc/cc3492?mf_ct_campaign=aol-synd-feed#:~:text=2011%20Louisiana%20Laws%20Civil%20Code,It)). For intentional damage (vandalism), you can sue for the damage cost – small claims won’t handle punitive damages, but you can get compensatory damages. If it was truly malicious, you might also have a separate criminal case (restitution can be ordered there, but you can still sue civilly). *Evidence:* Photos of the damage, receipts or estimates to fix/replace, and any witness statements. If the responsible party is a minor, you can sue the minor’s parents (parents are liable for minors’ damage in many cases). Louisiana Civil Code article 2318 makes parents liable for damage by their unemancipated minor children; you’d list the parent as defendant for a kid-caused damage. No special pre-suit requirement; just ensure you’re within a year. If the incident was part of a **pattern of harassment or a larger dispute**, note that small claims can only give money, not restraining orders – if you need an injunction to stop ongoing damage, you’d need a different court. But for a one-time damage event, small claims is fine.

- **Damage by a tenant to a landlord’s property:** If you’re a landlord and a former tenant (or exiting tenant) caused damage to the rental unit beyond normal wear and tear, you can sue for the cost of repairs. Often landlords try to withhold the security deposit for this – which can trigger the deposit return rules (see Landlord–Tenant section). If damages exceed the deposit or you didn’t collect one, a direct small claim might be needed. *Examples:* Tenant left holes in walls, broken windows, and trash – $3,000 to repair/clean. As landlord, you could sue for those actual damages. But caution: Louisiana’s **security deposit law** (La. R.S. 9:3251-3252) requires landlords to give an itemized statement of damages within **1 month** of lease end and return any remaining deposit ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=107468#:~:text=landlord%20or%20lessor%20to%20secure,are%20retained%20and%20giving%20the)). If you fail to do so, or willfully keep a deposit without sending that accounting, the tenant can actually sue *you* for penalties (double the deposit or $300, whichever greater) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=107469#:~:text=tenant%20or%20lessee%20the%20right,refund%20shall%20constitute%20willful%20failure)). So, ensure you followed that procedure. If you did and the tenant still owes beyond the deposit, you may use small claims to get the rest. Document the damages with a detailed list, photos from move-out, and receipts or estimates for repairs. Also bring the move-in checklist or lease clauses to differentiate “beyond normal wear.” There’s no special pre-suit demand required (the deposit law’s notice essentially serves that purpose). The prescriptive period is likely 3 years (damage to *immovable* property sometimes considered 1 year in tort, but since it arises from lease obligations, arguably 3 years for waste or 10 if contractual – to be safe, file within 1-3 years). This kind of claim might also be brought as a reconventional demand if the tenant sues you for the deposit – see Landlord–Tenant.

- **Damage by a landlord to a tenant’s property:** Conversely, if a landlord (or their workers) caused damage to your personal property – for instance, improper repairs led to water damage to your furniture, or the landlord’s negligence (like a leaking roof they failed to fix) ruined your belongings – you can sue the landlord for your property loss (up to $5k). *Example:* Repeated ceiling leaks (landlord knew of but delayed fixing) destroyed your $1,200 computer and $500 in books. This can be framed as negligence or breach of the lease warranty of habitability. Louisiana law requires landlords to keep premises in repair (Civil Code art. 2696) and they’re liable for damage caused by neglecting that duty. There’s also a general duty not to damage a tenant’s possessions when entering or working on the unit. *Pre-suit:* It helps if you gave written notice of the problem (like leak) and time to fix – bring copies of those notices to show the landlord was aware. If the damage was sudden (landlord’s repairman knocked over your TV), obviously just evidence of that incident. The prescriptive period is 1 year if you treat it as tort (most likely). Document your losses with receipts or valuations. Note: Check your renter’s insurance – if they paid you for the items, they might have subrogation rights (though unlikely to pursue in small claims). If you had no insurance or deductible, those are your direct losses to claim. No particular statutory cap or multiplier here; just actual damages. 

- **Tree/fence/boundary disputes resulting in damage:** Small claims can address property damage from neighbor disputes, like a neighbor’s tree falling and damaging your fence, or a dispute where one party destroys a shared fence. *Examples:* Neighbor’s tree limb (or entire tree) falls onto your shed – $2,000 damage; or a neighbor cut your hedges or trees without permission causing $1,500 loss; or they removed a survey stake or fence sections. You can sue for the repair or replacement value. Louisiana law on trees: if a neighbor’s tree falls due to natural causes (“Act of God” like a storm), generally the neighbor isn’t liable unless they knew it was hazardous. But if it was foreseeable or due to their negligence (e.g. they were cutting it and dropped it on your property), they are liable. For encroaching roots/branches, Louisiana allows you to trim to the property line, but if they cause damage (roots cracking your driveway), it’s arguable if the neighbor is liable – likely not unless negligence. Use small claims for clear instances of damage and let the judge allocate fault. *Boundary structures:* If you share a fence and the neighbor damaged it, or you paid to replace a shared fence and want contribution, you can seek up to $5k. Evidence: photos, maybe an arborist letter if tree was diseased and neighbor knew, repair estimates for the fence/shed. The prescriptive period for property damage is 1 year from the incident ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/2011/cc/cc3492?mf_ct_campaign=aol-synd-feed#:~:text=2011%20Louisiana%20Laws%20Civil%20Code,It)). *Special note:* If there’s an ongoing dispute about the *location of the boundary* or who owns a tree, that veers into property title issues, which small claims can’t resolve. Small claims will only deal with the money for damage. We cannot, for instance, decide a **boundary line** or order someone to move a fence (that’s injunctive relief). But it can award you money if the neighbor’s actions clearly caused you a loss.

- **Pet damage (dog bites dog, etc.):** If someone’s pet injures your pet or damages your property, you can sue the pet’s owner for vet bills or property repair. *Examples:* Your neighbor’s dog attacked your dog, leading to $800 in vet bills; or their dog chewed up your garden hose and cushions ($200 damage). In Louisiana, pet owners are generally liable for damage caused by their animals if they had reason to know the animal’s dangerous propensity or were negligent in controlling it (Civil Code art. 2321). For a straightforward dog-on-dog attack in a place dogs shouldn’t be running loose, the owner will likely be found negligent. Similarly, if a pet causes a car accident or such. The claim is a tort (one-year prescription from date of injury). There’s no special procedure – just evidence like vet reports, photos of injuries, maybe witness testimony that the other animal was unprovoked. If your *person* was bitten or injured, that often exceeds $5k in damages, but minor injuries could be in small claims (see Personal Injury below). For **pets as property**: Note that in law pets are property, so damages usually are economic (vet bills, etc.) rather than “pain and suffering” of the pet – you won’t get emotional distress in small claims for an injured or killed pet (those claims are very limited in any court). So keep it to tangible costs (treatment costs, market value if pet died, etc.). If a *person’s* dog was bitten and they had to get shots, that person’s medical costs could also be recovered (if under $5k, they could join that claim possibly – but likely separate, see PI). Ensure you identify the correct owner (if a minor owned the dog, sue the parents; if any insurance like homeowner covers it, they might step in, but you still sue the person). Pet damage suits are common in small claims.

- **Damage during a move (movers or friends helping):** If you hired movers and they damaged your furniture or walls, you can sue for the damage. *Example:* A moving company dropped your 65-inch TV, destroying it – $1,000 loss. Or a friend helping you move accidentally put a big gouge in your hardwood floor – you can claim the floor repair cost from that friend (though socially, you might hesitate – but legally you can). For **moving companies**, check your contract: many have liability limitations (like $0.60 per pound unless you bought insurance). Those limitations can be binding. But if the contract isn’t clear or you feel they were grossly negligent, you can test it in small claims. Interstate moves are governed by federal law (Carmack Amendment) which would preempt state claims and usually require written claim within 9 months and then federal court – so if it was an interstate move, small claims might be preempted. For local intrastate moves within Louisiana, state law and the contract apply. If the mover had an address in Louisiana, you can serve them here. If they’re an out-of-state mover and it was interstate, small claims may not have jurisdiction or be preempted, as mentioned. For friend/neighbor volunteers, it’s a straightforward negligence claim (they likely didn’t mean it, but still caused damage – some might use the defense that you assumed the risk by letting an amateur help, but legally they can still be liable). There’s no special requirement but obviously you could ask them to pay first. In small claims, evidence could be a repair quote for the floor, or the broken item itself or photos. One-year prescription from the date of move/damage. If suing a moving *company*, bring the bill of lading/contract and highlight the relevant sections; the judge can decide if the limitation clause is enforceable. Even if enforceable, you’d get the limited amount (maybe far less than actual value) – weigh if it’s worth it. If they were clearly at fault, sometimes companies will settle. Louisiana’s consumer law might not heavily regulate movers’ liability beyond contract terms, so it largely comes down to contract and negligence principles.

### Landlord–tenant (financial only — possession/eviction is separate)

*Small claims court **does not handle evictions or possession of real property** – those are handled as separate eviction proceedings (often as “summary proceedings” in City or Justice of the Peace courts, outside the small-claims division). But small claims **can handle money disputes** between landlords and tenants.* Common scenarios:

- **Unreturned security deposits:** This is perhaps the #1 small claims issue in landlord-tenant. In Louisiana, a landlord must return a residential lessee’s security deposit (or any remaining part after deductions) **within 1 month** after the lease ends ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=107468#:~:text=landlord%20or%20lessor%20to%20secure,are%20retained%20and%20giving%20the)). If they deduct anything, they must send an **itemized statement** of what they kept and why, within that same 1 month ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=107468#:~:text=residential%20or%20dwelling%20premises%20within,such%20statements%20may%20be%20sent)). If the landlord **willfully fails** to comply – meaning they don’t return the deposit or any statement at all, or they wrongfully keep it – the tenant can sue for **actual deposit wrongfully kept *plus* a penalty of $300 or double the amount wrongfully kept, whichever is greater** ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=107469#:~:text=tenant%20or%20lessee%20the%20right,refund%20shall%20constitute%20willful%20failure)). They can also get attorney fees (court can award them) in such cases. This is under La. R.S. 9:3251-3252, often called the Lessee’s Deposit Act. *Example:* You paid a $800 deposit. You gave your forwarding address. It’s been over 30 days since move-out and the landlord kept the whole deposit without sending any itemized list. You can sue in small claims for $800 (the deposit) + $800 penalty (double) = $1,600, and request court costs and maybe attorney fees ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=107469#:~:text=tenant%20or%20lessee%20the%20right,refund%20shall%20constitute%20willful%20failure)). Since $1,600 < $5k, it’s ideal for small claims. If the deposit were larger, say $2,500, double would be $5,000 – still within the cap (any more and it might exceed). **Tenant’s pre-suit steps:** You must furnish a forwarding address at end of lease for the landlord to send deposit/statement ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=107468#:~:text=advance%20or%20deposit%20is%20retained,such%20statements%20may%20be%20sent)). If you didn’t, the landlord’s obligation is somewhat in limbo (the law expects the tenant to give an address). But even if you forgot, if the landlord clearly knew how to reach you or could send to the unit and have it forwarded, you could argue they should attempt. Regardless, often tenants will send a **written demand** for the deposit once the 30 days expire. It’s smart to do so (via certified mail, referencing the statute). The law doesn’t explicitly require a demand to trigger penalties – the lapse of 30 days with willful retention is enough ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=107469#:~:text=of%20the%20security%20deposit%20wrongfully,refund%20shall%20constitute%20willful%20failure)). But a written demand helps prove willfulness. **Landlord’s defense:** The landlord can keep portions reasonably necessary to cover unpaid rent or damage beyond normal wear ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=107468#:~:text=residential%20or%20dwelling%20premises%20within,such%20statements%20may%20be%20sent)). If they did send an itemized list in time, the penalties likely don’t apply – then the dispute is just over whether those deductions were valid. Small claims judges will decide if charges (e.g. $100 for cleaning, $50 for a missing smoke detector) were reasonable. If the landlord complied with sending the list, the tenant can only recover the amount wrongfully withheld (no penalty). If landlord gave no list, penalty likely applies. Evidence: For tenants, bring lease, proof of deposit amount, proof of giving keys and forwarding address, and absence of any mailed itemization (or the envelope if they mailed after 30 days). For condition, photos of how you left the place can rebut claimed damages. **Prescription:** The statute doesn’t specify, but general contract or statutory penalty actions would likely be 3 years. It’s wise to sue within 1-2 years to be safe. This is a very common and suitable small claim.

- **Refund of prepaid last month’s rent:** If you paid “first and last month’s rent” upfront and then didn’t end up staying the last month (or you properly terminated early and the landlord re-rented), you might be owed a refund of that last month’s rent. *Example:* You paid $1,200 last month in advance, but you left early and the landlord immediately had a new tenant start – thus they double-collected for that month. You can sue for the $1,200 double-paid. Or if you left mid-month and the landlord kept rent for the unused portion without justification. These would be handled like any other contract refund issue. No special statute other than basic contract law – if the landlord had no damages and unjustly kept your money, you should get it back. Ensure you *did* follow any notice requirements when leaving early; if you broke the lease, the landlord might be entitled to keep rent for the notice period or until re-letting. But if they had no gap, you have a strong argument for refund (the law doesn’t allow double-dipping). This could also be phrased as unjust enrichment. Prescription likely 3 years (maybe 10 if written lease). Document the timeline of move-out and new occupancy (maybe neighbor statements or ads showing it was re-rented). Often, these get rolled into deposit disputes too.

- **Habitability / repair-and-deduct counter-claims:** Small claims can also handle situations where a tenant fixed something themselves or suffered from unlivable conditions and seeks compensation. *Example:* The landlord refused to fix an AC for two months in summer, so you paid $300 for an AC repair and want to deduct that from rent (or get reimbursed because you ended up paying rent in full). In Louisiana, the warranty of habitability (Civil Code art. 2696) lets tenants sometimes repair and deduct or claim a rent reduction for the period of uninhabitability. There’s not a very specific statute like some states, but jurisprudence allows it. In small claims, this often arises *defensively*: the landlord might sue for unpaid rent, and the tenant counterclaims for reductions due to bad conditions. If you as a tenant want to affirmatively recover money (say you moved out because of terrible conditions and want back some rent paid), you can file that as a claim for breach of warranty. Judges can award rent abatements or refunds for periods where essentials (water, heat, etc.) were not provided. There’s no special pre-suit requirement but showing that you notified the landlord of issues and gave them a chance to fix is crucial. If you did *repair and deduct*, bring evidence of the cost and that it was a necessary repair the landlord failed to do timely. The law implicitly requires that the repair be necessary and the cost reasonable. Small claims judges are likely to offset the amounts. Keep in mind, if this is part of an ongoing lease and you still live there, doing a direct lawsuit might sour things – these issues sometimes are resolved in eviction court or by negotiation. But legally you can bring it. Timeline: Such claims should be brought within 1 year of the issue if seen as tortious, or possibly longer if contractual, but best not to wait long (it could look waived if you stayed and paid without action for long).

- **Unpaid rent or damages by landlord against a former tenant:** Landlords can also use small claims to pursue ex-tenants for **unpaid rent** (discussed in Contract disputes above) or for **property damage beyond normal wear** (discussed under Property damage). Often, though, these get handled by applying the deposit or in eviction court if the eviction happened. But if, for example, a tenant left owing 2 months’ rent plus $1,000 in damages above the deposit, the landlord can sue for those sums in small claims (provided the total ≤ $5k). We covered unpaid rent (3-year prescription, straightforward) and property damage (1-year or so, maybe 3 if under lease obligations). One caveat: If the tenant **filed bankruptcy** after moving out, that debt might be discharged or the suit barred by the automatic stay – a landlord should verify before suing. Also, ensure you’re suing the *correct parties* – all tenants on the lease or co-signers if any, and have a current address to serve them. It’s wise for landlords to have kept a forwarding address or the rental application info. No special demand needed, but providing a final account statement to the tenant is good practice.

- **Wrongful retention of tenant’s property after move-out:** If a landlord or property manager kept or disposed of belongings you left behind without following legal procedures, you might have a claim. Louisiana law (Civil Code art. 2708) allows a landlord to dispose of *abandoned* property after delivery of possession, but if the items were not truly abandoned (e.g. you were coming back for them or the landlord locked you out and prevented retrieval), the landlord could be liable. *Example:* You were evicted or moved out, and the landlord immediately threw out your furniture worth $2,000 without giving you a chance to get it. You could sue for the value of the belongings. The success may depend on circumstances – if there was a formal eviction with a sheriff, usually the tenant must remove property or it’s considered abandoned. If informal, a landlord should give a short grace period. Some cities might have ordinances on this. In small claims, it’s simply a property damage/loss claim. Show proof of the items’ value (photos, receipts). Landlord defenses: “it was trash/abandoned” – the judge will fact-find. If the landlord clearly acted too quickly or intentionally withheld items (“lien” for rent without legal right), they could owe you. There’s no set penalty statute (except possibly if it violated deposit law by holding items hostage, but not really). It’s just actual value. Do note if you still owed rent, some landlords think they can hold property as collateral – that’s not legal in Louisiana without a court judgment (self-help seizure is not allowed except through proper lessor’s privilege procedures via court). So you could emphasize that. The prescriptive period likely 1 year from losing the items (conversion is a tort). 

- **Dispute over fees (cleaning fees, late fees, pet fees, etc.):** Many leases have assorted fees. If you as a tenant were charged unreasonable fees upon move-out (e.g. $300 “cleaning fee”) and the landlord deducted that from your deposit or demands you pay it, you can dispute that in small claims. Typically this comes up as part of a deposit suit – you’d argue that withheld fee was not allowed. Louisiana law doesn’t specifically prohibit cleaning fees if provided in lease, but any fee that is punitive might be scrutinized. Judges often allow reasonable cleaning costs if unit was dirty, but if a landlord automatically charges a non-refundable cleaning fee regardless of condition, some might consider that the tenant’s money unless truly agreed. Similarly, if a landlord is suing you for a bunch of late fees or “lease-break fee,” you can defend and/or counterclaim if you overpaid. Small claims can resolve these by looking at the lease terms and fairness. *Example:* Lease says $50 late fee per day – you accumulated $500 in late fees, which is exorbitant relative to rent. A judge might refuse to enforce an unconscionable amount and only allow a portion. There’s not a clear line – it’s up to the court’s equitable powers. If you already paid such a fee and think it was wrongfully taken, you could sue to recover it (subject possibly to 3-year prescription for debt/payment disputes). Provide the lease and proof of payment. Landlords likewise can sue for legitimate fees in the lease that the tenant didn’t pay (like a bounced check fee – Louisiana allows NSF fee up to $25 or 5% by statute ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=B,the%20location%20will%20see%20it))). Those would be part of a claim for rent or damages.

### Employment & wages

- **Unpaid final paycheck / earned wages:** If your employer didn’t pay your last paycheck or other earned wages, you can absolutely use small claims (assuming it’s a private employment and not preempted by federal law or requiring a labor agency handling – more on that below). Louisiana law (La. R.S. 23:631-632) requires employers to pay a discharged employee **all due wages by the next regular payday or within 15 days, whichever is earlier** ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=83945#:~:text=kind%20whatever%2C%20it%20shall%20be,of%20discharge%2C%20whichever%20occurs%20first)). If an employee quits, the same timeline applies by next payday or 15 days ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=83945#:~:text=whichever%20occurs%20first)). If the employer fails to pay without a good-faith dispute, they owe penalty wages of **up to 90 days’ pay** (or for the days until they pay, whichever is less) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)), plus attorney fees ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=C,demand%20following%20discharge%20or%20resignation)). This is often called “waiting time penalties” similar to California’s law. *Example:* You were fired and the company didn’t send your last paycheck (for say $800). It’s now 30 days later. You can sue for $800 + 90 days of your daily wage as penalty (e.g. if you made $100/day, that’s $9,000, but the statute caps it at 90 days of wages or until paid, whichever is less) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)). Note: That $9,000 clearly exceeds small claims $5k – so you’d have to **waive** any excess penalty and claim up to $5,000, or file in a higher court. In practice, many final wage disputes in small claims involve a smaller penalty because the daily wage or delay isn’t huge. For instance, if your daily wage was $50 and they delayed 30 days, penalty = $1,500, plus wages $800 = $2,300 total. That fits. **Procedure:** You must **make demand** for your wages after termination ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=well,demand%20following%20discharge%20or%20resignation)) – the law says if wages weren’t paid within 3 days after due, a “well-founded suit” gets penalties and fees ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=C,demand%20following%20discharge%20or%20resignation)). So at least verbally, but better in writing, demand your paycheck. Once in small claims, cite R.S. 23:632 for penalties ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)) and R.S. 23:631 for the duty ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=83945#:~:text=the%20employment%20is%20by%20the,of%20discharge%2C%20whichever%20occurs%20first)). The employer’s defense can be “good-faith dispute” – if they honestly thought you weren’t due that money (maybe you didn’t return company property, etc.), a judge can deny penalties ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=B,Subsection%20A%20of%20this%20Section)). But if they just stalled or had no excuse, penalty wages are often awarded. You also get **attorney fees** if you win any amount of unpaid wages ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=C,demand%20following%20discharge%20or%20resignation)) – in small claims, since likely no attorney, you might not get fees (some judges might award a small amount for your efforts or costs). The prescriptive period for wage claims under this statute is often considered 3 years (as it’s a liability created by statute or just unpaid compensation – which falls under 3-year for wages ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages))). So you have up to 3 years, but penalty portion might be argued as well (some cases treat penalty as penal and one-year, but generally it’s part of wage claim remedy). It’s safest to file within 1 year to avoid any complexity. Also note: You can alternatively file a wage claim with the **Louisiana Workforce Commission** (LWC) – they have an administrative process to assist employees. However, that process is basically the LWC contacting the employer; they don’t hold a hearing or issue orders like some states. So small claims court is often your best bet for quick resolution if the amount is moderate. (If it were a larger wage claim, sometimes employees sue in district court or as a class if systemic.)

- **Unpaid commissions or bonuses:** If you earned commissions or a bonus under a compensation plan and the employer didn’t pay, you can sue for those as wages (assuming they were vested/earned per the company policy). Louisiana’s wage statutes include “commissions” in the definition of wages. For commissions, a key issue is whether the commission was due **during employment or at termination** and if any conditions apply (e.g. must be employed on payment date). If you met the conditions, it’s due. *Example:* You sold a big account, which under your plan yields a $2,000 commission, but you resigned before the payout date and the company refuses to pay. If your contract doesn’t clearly forfeit it, you might claim it. There have been Louisiana cases on whether commissions after termination are due – it often depends on the agreement. For **bonuses**, if it was discretionary, you may have a harder case (discretionary bonuses are not “wages” that can be demanded). But if it was a promised measurable bonus (like “if we hit X target, you get $500”), that’s arguably earned wages. The wage statute’s penalties would apply to earned commissions just like wages, if willfully not paid ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)). Pre-suit: make a clear demand in writing, and cite any written commission plan or emails where managers confirmed you earned it. Small claims judges will look at fairness and the written terms. If the policy is ambiguous, they often side with the employee for earned pay. Ensure it’s within 3 years (for wages), though arguably it could be seen as breach of contract (10 years) – but treat as wage claim with 3-year to be safe.

- **Unreimbursed business expenses:** If your employer owes you for expense reimbursements (mileage, travel costs, purchases made for work) and hasn’t paid, you can claim those. They’re not exactly “wages” under the statute (so penalty wages might not apply unless possibly if an expense allowance is considered part of wages). But it’s a straightforward contract claim – the company agreed (explicitly or implicitly by policy) to repay authorized business expenses. *Example:* You spent $300 on supplies for work on your personal card with approval, and the company never paid you back. Or you have $1,000 of unpaid mileage claims. You’d sue for those sums. Provide documentation: expense reports, receipts, any written approval. There’s no penalty statute specific to expenses, but you might recover interest from date due. The prescription likely 3 years (as an employment-related payment) or could be 10 if based on written policy. This is generally a smaller category and fits small claims well for quick resolution.

- **Misclassification disputes (if recoverable as money):** If you were treated as an independent contractor but believe you were an employee and thus entitled to certain benefits or overtime, small claims might **not** be the ideal forum for the classification determination itself (that can be complex). However, there is one angle: If, due to misclassification, you weren’t paid overtime or minimum wage that you should have been, you might claim those unpaid wages. But note: **Overtime and minimum wage** are governed by the federal Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA). The FLSA allows you to sue in court (including state court) for unpaid overtime/minimum wage, and to recover double damages (liquidated damages) and attorney fees. But such suits can’t be brought in small claims if they require application of federal law? Actually, state courts have concurrent jurisdiction for FLSA, so you could sue for e.g. $2,000 unpaid overtime in small claims. The issue is small claims judges may not be well-versed in FLSA complexities (they’d likely try anyway). If you bring an FLSA claim, mention the law entitles double damages and fees – but double might push you over $5k if close. You could waive liquidated damages to fit. *Example:* You were called an independent contractor, paid straight time $15/hour for 50 hours/week – effectively you have 10 hours of overtime each week unpaid (at time-and-a-half, you’re short $75/week). Over months that might be $1,000 owed. You can sue for that $1,000. The misclassification aspect will come into play because the employer will say you’re not entitled since you were a contractor. The court will apply the economic realities test to see if you were essentially an employee. It’s a bit advanced for small claims but not impossible to argue with facts (control, schedule, etc.). If you prove you should’ve been treated as an employee, they owe the wages. *Better forum:* Louisiana Workforce Commission or U.S. Dept of Labor sometimes handle such wage claims, but usually they focus on bigger issues or multiple employees. For a small amount, small claims may be fine. If it’s a matter of **employee benefits** (like you were misclassified so you missed out on insurance or vacation pay), those are tougher to quantify or may be governed by ERISA (a federal law that would be outside small claims). So stick to wage-type of demands. *Pre-suit:* No special step, but ensure you have records of hours worked and pay received. The prescriptive period under FLSA is 2 years (3 if willful violation). Under state law, if you frame it as a Louisiana wage claim (like not paying earned wages) could be 3 years. But keep 2 years in mind for overtime. Because of complexity, if over a couple thousand, some might get an attorney (attorney fees can be awarded). But for a DIY approach under $5k, small claims can work.

- **Note on Louisiana Labor Dept vs. court:** Louisiana does not have a standalone Labor Commissioner hearing process like California. The **Louisiana Workforce Commission (LWC)** has a wage claim form you can file, and they will contact the employer and attempt to collect. They can also assess penalties under R.S. 23:632 administratively. However, the LWC cannot hold a hearing or force payment beyond sending notices. If the employer ignores them, they typically advise you to sue. Small Claims Court gives you an enforceable judgment. So, for most wage issues under $5,000, **small claims is the effective route**. Reserve LWC involvement for potential extra pressure or if you’re unable to locate the employer (they might help). If the employer is out of state, small claims might have trouble with jurisdiction; in that case, contacting federal DOL could be an option if it’s FLSA, or suing in the employer’s state.
