For example, you can’t sue someone for breaking a law and demand they pay a fine to you. You can only sue for *your* damages. (Exception: if a statute says explicitly you as an aggrieved party get a certain penalty – like NSF checks double, or LUTPA treble with notice – those we covered because they are your damages by statute). But in general, if it’s a law violation that didn’t cause you personal loss, you have no standing to sue in small claims.

In short, **small claims is inappropriate for:** evictions, family matters, class actions, injunctions, any case needing extensive equitable relief or declaration of rights/status, and cases against immune or specialized defendants. Louisiana law explicitly removed **summary proceedings, class actions, and executory proceedings** from small claims ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=appropriate%20relief%2C%20including%20money%20damages,executory%20proceedings%20shall%20be%20prohibited)) (summary proceedings include evictions, attachments, garnishments as initial actions, etc.; executory proceedings are a type of foreclosure).

If you aren’t sure if your case is allowed: ask the clerk. They often will tell you if it’s not accepted (e.g., “we can’t take that filing here”). It’s better to err on side of not filing those, because if you do, the judge at best will dismiss it (without prejudice perhaps) and you wasted time.

### Damages — what you can and cannot recover in small claims

When you win a small claims case in Louisiana, the court can award **monetary damages** to compensate you. Here’s what can (and cannot) be part of your judgment:

- **Compensatory damages:** These are the bread-and-butter – reimbursement for **out-of-pocket losses, costs, or the value of property** lost/damaged. Small claims judges will award actual damages you prove, such as the amount you paid and didn’t get benefit from, the cost to repair something, medical bills from an injury, lost wages from time you couldn’t work (if directly due to defendant’s act), etc. Essentially, any economic loss that is a direct result of the wrong. Make sure to bring receipts, estimates, invoices, or other evidence to substantiate amounts. Louisiana requires that a judgment be founded on “competent evidence” ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=are%20relaxed%2C%20and%20all%20relevant,is%20founded%20upon%20competent%20evidence)) even if hearsay is allowed – the judge won’t just take a guess with no proof. So, for example, if suing for car damage, have the repair estimate or Blue Book printout for value. If you’re claiming lost income, bring pay stubs or a letter from employer on what you lost. **Note:** Louisiana is a civil law state that doesn’t categorize damages differently than common law in terms of how small claims handles them, so **compensatory** covers both **special damages** (specific monetary losses) and potentially **general damages** (like pain and suffering, emotional distress). General damages are compensatory too, but intangible.

- **Pain and suffering / emotional distress (general damages):** In small claims, judges are cautious about awarding these because they are subjective. However, they *can* be awarded if the case merits and the total stays within the $5k cap. For instance, in a minor personal injury case, a judge might award a couple thousand for pain in addition to medical costs if that still fits under $5k. There’s no statutory prohibition on punitive or exemplary damages (see below) specifically says but for punitive see below, but **general damages** (pain, suffering, inconvenience) are allowed as part of compensatory damages. Just be reasonable because if they seem speculative or too high a portion of the claim, a judge might limit them or not give any, focusing on actual expenses. If you do claim them, you should articulate clearly what the suffering was (e.g., "I endured significant pain for two weeks, could not sleep well, and missed out on leisure activities" or "the humiliation caused by the defamatory statement impacted me for months"). It’s up to the judge’s discretion and they often err on conservative side. Also remember if you have large general damages, likely your case is too big for small claims anyway.

- **Punitive/exemplary damages:** As mentioned, Louisiana generally does not permit punitive damages unless a specific statute allows it (and those are rare circumstances like DUI injuries). In small claims, you almost never have a scenario that qualifies (and if it did, the value is high and not suited for $5k court). **Small claims judges will not award punitive damages** on their own, because they adhere to Louisiana law – which disallows punitive damages except e.g. R.S. 14:   (drunk driving). Even if you try to label something punitive (like "I want triple damages to punish them"), the judge cannot grant that unless a statute like LUTPA or bad check law or treble timber law explicitly calls for multiple. Those are actually considered *statutory damages or enhanced compensatory* rather than "punitive" in LA. So in general, **no punitive damages**. 

- **Statutory multipliers or penalties:** If a Louisiana statute provides for a multiple of damages or a fixed penalty, small claims can award that because it’s part of the plaintiff’s legal right. For example, double damages on a bad check ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=A,attorney%20fees%20and%20court%20costs)), 90-days wages penalty for late paycheck ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)), $2000 statutory damages under some consumer law, etc. These are not considered “punitive” in the forbidden sense but a legislatively-authorized remedy. So yes, a small claims judge can grant those if proven. Always cite the statute to educate the judge. E.g., "Under R.S. 9:3252, since my landlord kept my deposit willfully with no itemization, I’m entitled to twice the wrongfully withheld amount or $300, whichever is greater" ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=107469#:~:text=tenant%20or%20lessee%20the%20right,refund%20shall%20constitute%20willful%20failure)). Or "Under R.S. 23:632, I seek 90 days of penalty wages for late payment" ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=A,lesser%20amount%20of%20penalty%20wages)). Provided the total doesn’t exceed $5k, these are allowable. If the statute would yield more than $5k, you’d have to cap your ask at $5k. The judge can’t exceed $5k anyway.

- **Attorney’s fees and court costs:** In Louisiana, **court costs** are generally awarded to the prevailing party as a matter of course (the judge usually will state that or it’s implied by law). That means your filing fee, service fees, and subpoena fees can be added to the judgment against the losing party ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77964#:~:text=B,Louisiana%20Code%20of%20Civil%20Procedure)) (R.S. 13:5205(B) notes costs may be waived for indigent, but doesn’t override the general principle that costs are taxed to loser). So yes, if you win, ask politely “Your Honor, I’d also request court costs.” They usually say granted, and the clerk will calculate them. Court costs in small claims include the initial filing fee (e.g. $35) and any additional service fees you paid (e.g. $20 for serving defendant) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77964#:~:text=A,of%20process%20is%20required%20to)), plus perhaps costs of mailing cert mail or subpoena fees if any. These are typically relatively small (tens of dollars), but important. **Attorney’s fees:** Louisiana follows the rule that each party pays their own attorney unless a contract or statute provides otherwise. In small claims, generally, **you cannot recover attorney’s fees** *unless* there’s a specific law or contract clause that awards them. Examples of when you can: Open account suits (with proper demand) allow reasonable attorney fees ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/law.aspx?d=107205#:~:text=A,on%20the%20claim%20is%20rendered)), security deposit bad-faith retention allows attorney fees to tenant (court "shall" award) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=C,demand%20following%20discharge%20or%20resignation)), final wages cases allow attorney fees to employee ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=83946#:~:text=C,demand%20following%20discharge%20or%20resignation)), LUTPA allows attorney fees to prevailing consumer ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/LawPrint.aspx?d=104033#:~:text=notice%20by%20the%20attorney%20general%2C,reasonable%20attorney%20fees%20and%20costs)), NSF check law allows attorney fees ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-9/rs-9-2782/#:~:text=registered%20mail%2C%20the%20drawer%20shall,attorney%20fees%20and%20court%20costs)), etc. If you invoke one of those, you can ask for a certain amount of attorney fees. But note: in small claims, often plaintiffs don’t have an actual attorney – can they still get fees? Usually not, because it’s meant to reimburse actual attorney expense. If you did consult or have limited representation, you’d have to show some basis (like a billing or something). Some judges might award a nominal “attorney fee” to self-represented wins under these statutes ($100 or so) but legally pro se litigants are not entitled to attorney fees (because you didn’t incur attorney cost). There’s no clear rule for small claims pro se with an attorney-fee statute – likely they won’t give it. If you did hire a lawyer to help draft or appear (rare in small claims since lawyers are allowed though), then you would ask for the reasonable fee. The judge must award if statute says “shall award” (like deposit or wages) – they might ask the attorney to submit an affidavit of time. If contract has an attorney fee clause (like many promissory notes say “if collected by attorney, 10% fee”), the judge can include a reasonable fee – not automatically the full 10% maybe, but often they honor what contract says if not excessive. But again, if no actual attorney was needed because you handled it, they may not enforce that clause, or just minimal. So bottom line: **attorney fees recoverable only when provided by law or contract**, and even then, typically only if you actually paid or owe an attorney. Court costs, however, you get by default as winner (and they don’t count toward the $5k cap typically – the $5k cap is usually on the claim amount exclusive of court costs and possibly interest).

- **Interest (pre-judgment and post-judgment):** 
    - **Pre-judgment interest:** In Louisiana, on certain types of damages, interest runs from date of loss (“judicial demand”) automatically by law. Specifically, tort damages (personal injury, property damage, etc., “ex delicto”) incur **legal interest from the date the suit is filed** ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77703#:~:text=,may%20be%20rendered%20by%20any)). Contractual claims (“ex contractu”) typically incur interest from when judgment is rendered, unless the claim itself was for a sum due on a certain date (like a note with interest or an open account – those often interest from default due date). It can get technical, but simple guidance: If you had a contractual debt with an agreed interest rate (like 5% per annum on a loan), you can claim that interest up to judgment as part of damages (subject to cap). If none specifically, you might still be able to ask for **legal interest** (the statutory rate, which in LA is variable yearly). Louisiana’s legal interest rate is set each year (e.g. 2024 it was 8.75% ([www.lsba.org](https://www.lsba.org/NewsArticle.aspx?Article=4ed79a25-82ef-4767-9ee2-f898160ed07d#:~:text=2024%20Judicial%20Interest%20Rate%20is,interest%20for%20calendar%20year%202024)), 2025 8.25%, 2026 7.5% ([ofi.la.gov](https://ofi.la.gov/legal/statutes-rules-policies-opinions/judicial-interest-rates/#:~:text=Judicial%20Interest%20Rates%20Legal%20%2F%2F,2024%20to%20December%2031%2C%202024))). Unless you specifically plead it, a small claims judgment might not automatically mention pre-judgment interest. But you can certainly request it. Judges in Louisiana know that *if the law allows, interest attaches by law.* They might simply say “with legal interest from date of judicial demand.” For tort cases, that’s by statute (La. R.S. 13:4203) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77703#:~:text=,may%20be%20rendered%20by%20any)) – interest from petition date. For contract cases, La. Civ Code 2000 allows interest if stipulated or from default on money obligation, otherwise interest from judicial demand might be discretionary. Many plaintiffs in small claims don’t bother with interest if the case is resolved relatively quick (the interest on a $2,000 claim over, say, 6 months at ~8% is under $80). But if it’s been years or a bigger sum, absolutely ask. So **yes, interest can be included**. It does not count against the $5,000 cap; interest is considered separate as per statute – the $5k refers to “amount in dispute… exclusive of interest” ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=A,the%20Code%20of%20Civil%20Procedure)). So the judge can award principal $5,000 plus interest on that from suit date.
    - **Post-judgment interest:** Once you get a judgment, it accrues interest at the judicial rate until paid. Louisiana sets an annual rate each year (7.5% for 2026) ([ofi.la.gov](https://ofi.la.gov/legal/statutes-rules-policies-opinions/judicial-interest-rates/#:~:text=Judicial%20Interest%20Rates%20Legal%20%2F%2F,2024%20to%20December%2031%2C%202024)). By law, *all judgments* in Louisiana bear interest from the date of judgment until satisfied (civil Code art. 3500 or R.S. 13:4202) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77702#:~:text=,September%2012%2C%201980%2C%20the%20rate)). The judgment form might or might not explicitly say it, but it’s implied by law if not. Often small claims judgments will note “with legal interest from date of judgment” if not already from earlier. Either way, you are entitled to that. So if the defendant drags their feet paying, you earn interest. The interest rate can change Jan 1 each year (the rate at time period apply for that period). This is a strong motivator for prompt payment if it’s high. If you want clarity, you can ask the judge to include: “... together with legal interest from date of (demand or judgment) until paid, and all court costs.” That covers it. They usually oblige.

- **Filing fees and service fees as recoverable costs:** Yes, as said, those are part of costs. R.S. 13:5205 even ties the fees to small claims; initial $35 plaintiff pays can be recovered from defendant if plaintiff wins, and any extra service or subpoena fees up to $20 each you paid can be accounted ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77964#:~:text=A,of%20process%20is%20required%20to)). You typically don’t have to ask; the clerk will add costs to the total when preparing judgment for signature. But double-check the judgment includes something like “and costs of court.” If not, you might move to amend. It’s routine.

- **What about other costs like lost time or travel?** Generally, no, you cannot claim your own time spent preparing or coming to court as damages (unless it’s directly part of the claim, e.g. you had to rent a car because of their fault, that rental is a damage). But time spent litigating is not awarded except via attorney fee statute if you had an attorney. If you took off work to attend trial, that’s considered part of the burden of suing – courts don’t usually award that as damages.

In summary, small claims can award you **all your actual economic losses** and any **statutory enhancements** applicable, subject to the $5,000 limit (for principal damages). **Punitive damages are not available** except via statutory double/treble which we treat as compensatory under law. **Attorney fees** are only if a statute or contract calls for them and typically only to extent you actually incurred them. **Interest** is recoverable both pre- (if law provides) and post-judgment (automatically by law), and **court costs** definitely are recoverable.

## 3. Where to file

**Court name:** In Louisiana, small claims cases are heard in either the **City Court** or **Justice of the Peace Court** for the locality. Louisiana does *not* have a single unified “Small Claims Court” statewide; instead, small claims is a division or program within existing courts. Specifically:
- In cities and larger towns, the **City Court** (or Parish Court in some parishes) typically has a **Small Claims Division** ([lasc.libguides.com](https://lasc.libguides.com/c.php?g=463399&p=3168105#:~:text=%C2%A75201)). For example, Baton Rouge City Court – Small Claims Section; Shreveport City Court – Small Claims; Orleans Parish has First City Court (which handles small claims on the East Bank) and Second City Court (Algiers) with their own small claims processes.
- In rural areas or places without a city court, the local **Justice of the Peace (JP) Court** handles small civil claims. JP courts are the small local courts in each ward or district of a parish. They have jurisdiction over small claims up to $5,000 as well ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=112118#:~:text=A,not%20exceed%20five%20thousand%20dollars)). So if you’re outside a city’s limits and there’s a JP for your ward, you can file there.

To determine where *exactly* to file:
  - **If your parish has a City Court covering the area where the defendant lives or the dispute arose**, you’ll likely file in that City Court’s small claims division. For example:
     - East Baton Rouge Parish: Baton Rouge City Court (covers city and parish-wide for small claims).
     - Orleans Parish: First City Court (covers east bank of Orleans, and Second City Court for west bank).
     - Jefferson Parish: It doesn’t have “city courts” because not incorporated the same, but it has *First Parish Court* (Metairie area) and *Second Parish Court* (Gretna area) which function similarly – they have small claims jurisdiction by local rule for $5k cases.
     - Caddo Parish: Shreveport City Court for within city, outside city maybe JPs.
     - Same for Lafayette (Lafayette City Court), Calcasieu (Lake Charles City Court), Ouachita (Monroe City Court), etc.
  - **If no City/Parish Court with small claims exists for the locale**, use the Justice of the Peace. Each parish is divided into Justice of the Peace districts (wards). The JP court has concurrent jurisdiction up to $5k ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=112118#:~:text=A,not%20exceed%20five%20thousand%20dollars)). Often, the rule of thumb: If the defendant lives outside city limits, or incident happened there, go to the JP of that ward if available.

**Venue rules (location selection):** You must file in a court that is a proper “venue” for the case or risk dismissal or transfer. In Louisiana, venue for ordinary civil suits is governed by Code of Civil Procedure arts. 42 and 74 etc., and those largely apply to small claims too. Key venue guidelines:
  - **Individual defendant’s domicile:** Generally, file in the parish (and court) where the **defendant lives**. That’s usually the preferred venue ([www.nolo.com](https://www.nolo.com/legal-encyclopedia/louisiana-small-claims-court-31722.html#:~:text=divisions%2C%20but%20you%20can%27t%20always,transfer%20or%20dismiss%20your%20action)). For small claims, this often means where defendant’s address is. E.g. if the person you’re suing lives in East Baton Rouge, you’d file in EBR’s city court (or JP if outside city).
  - **Business defendant:** If suing a business:
      - If it’s a company with a registered office, venue can be where its **registered office** or principal business establishment is. E.g., to sue a LLC, you could file where its headquarters is (check La Secretary of State records for address).
      - If an out-of-state company, venue might be where you interacted with it or where it does business in Louisiana.
  - **Contract cases:** You can also file in the parish **where the contract was made or was to be performed** (this is per CCP art. 76.1). So if you entered a service contract in one parish, that could be venue. For example, if you and the defendant signed or orally agreed in Parish A, you can sue in Parish A even if defendant moved. Or if work was done in Parish B, that might be a venue as “place of performance.”

content.text:
## 1. Jurisdictional limits — MAXIMUM AMOUNT YOU CAN SUE FOR

**Dollar cap:** Louisiana small claims divisions can hear cases for money disputes up to **$5,000** (five thousand dollars)[1], **exclusive of interest, court costs, attorney fees, or statutory penalties.** This $5,000 limit is the same regardless of who the plaintiff is – **individuals, businesses, and organizations** are all subject to the $5k cap. You cannot raise the cap by splitting one claim into multiple suits (courts will not allow “claim splitting” – the entire claim should be sued for at once). Moreover, **no more than 10 plaintiffs** can be joined in one small claim suit, and **class actions are not allowed** in small claims court[1]. If you have a claim over $5,000, you would need to **waive (give up)** the amount above $5,000 and sue for only $5,000, or file your case in a higher court (City/Parish Court or District Court) to seek the full amount.

**Types of relief:** Louisiana’s small claims court can award **money damages** and even some equitable relief incidental to a money dispute (such as canceling a contract or ordering property to be returned). However, **injunctions or restraining orders** (court orders to do or not do something) **cannot be issued** in small claims, except an order to suspend execution of its own judgment if necessary[2]. Also, **“summary proceedings”** (like evictions or other expedited cases) and **executory proceedings** (like foreclosures) are *not* handled in small claims[2] – those must be filed in the appropriate regular court section. **Multiple cases:** If a person or company files a large volume of small claims, the court may impose **mass filing limits by local rule** (for example, a court might limit how many small claims one party can file per week or require special permission beyond a certain number)[3]. This aims to prevent abuse by high‐volume plaintiffs (like collection agencies), though not all courts have such rules on the books.

**Geographic coverage:** Small claims cases in Louisiana are handled within existing courts. Each *City Court* is authorized to establish a small claims division for its territory. In areas without a city court, small claims are handled by the local *Justice of the Peace* court. The jurisdictional dollar limit is generally **$5,000 across the state**. (One minor exception: in the City Court of East St. Tammany, the statute ties its small claims limit to the Justice of the Peace limit, which is also $5,000 – effectively the same currently[4].) 

**No jury; informal procedure:** Small claims cases are **heard by a judge or magistrate**, not a jury. The proceedings are relatively informal. The Louisiana statute specifically says small claims hearings should be conducted to do “substantial justice” and are **not bound by the normal rules of evidence or procedure** (except for privileges)[5]. This means the judge can consider hearsay or other informal evidence if it seems reliable, and the court can relax procedural technicalities to get to a fair outcome. (For example, you can simply tell your story in your own words and present receipts or photographs, rather than needing formal witness examinations.) The judge may also actively ask questions to each side to establish the facts. Each City Court can also **limit the role of attorneys by court rule** in small claims cases[6]. In many small claims courts, **attorneys are allowed** to appear, but because of the simple format most people represent themselves – and some courts may discourage or restrict active participation by lawyers to keep things informal[6]. 

**Parties:** You can sue or be sued in small claims as an **individual person** (including minors through a legal guardian), a **business (corporation or LLC)**, a **partnership**, or other legal entity. Out-of-state companies can use Louisiana small claims if the court has jurisdiction (e.g. the dispute happened in Louisiana), although an out-of-state business might need to ensure it’s properly registered in Louisiana to prosecute a claim. Government entities (like a city or the State of Louisiana) generally do *not* file small claims – and if you have a claim against a government body, it likely must go through a different process (see “What you CANNOT sue for”). Also, **no class action suits** can be brought in small claims – each claim must be by an individual plaintiff for their own loss[1].

**Appeals:** One unique aspect – in Louisiana, **by choosing small claims court, the plaintiff *waives the right to appeal*** the judgment[7]. The defendant also **waives appeal** *unless* they take steps *before trial* to transfer the case out of small claims. (In other words, a small claims judgment is final and binding for both sides if it remains in the small claims division – there is no routine appeal on the verdict itself.) A defendant who wishes to preserve a right to appeal or needs to claim more than $5,000 can file a **motion to remove the case to the court’s regular docket** within the time allowed to answer[7]. If that motion is filed, the case is transferred to the ordinary civil court procedure (and any appeal would then follow the normal appeal rules of that court). But if no such removal is requested, the small claims judgment will become final (after any brief new-trial period) with **no appeal** for either side. This is different from many other states, so be aware: as a **plaintiff**, you are voluntarily giving up any appeal when you file in small claims – you’re choosing a one-and-done, expedited process. As a defendant, if you trust the small claims judge and the amount is modest, you might not object – but if you strongly contest liability or have a large counterclaim, you must timely remove (or in some courts, **opt out**) to preserve a conventional trial and appeal route.

*(Note:* “Removal” in this context is a request to the **City Court’s** normal civil section – not to a federal court. The small claim simply gets transferred to the city court’s regular civil docket or to District Court if appropriate. The law says the motion to transfer out **“shall be granted forthwith”** if timely filed[7], and the case will proceed as a normal civil suit.)

## 2. What types of cases qualify — EVERYTHING YOU CAN SUE FOR

Louisiana’s small claims courts can handle a wide variety of disputes as long as the **primary relief sought is money (damages) not exceeding $5,000**. Below is an **exhaustive list** of case types that *can* be brought in small claims – with examples and any Louisiana-specific rules or quirks to note. If your situation fits one of these categories and the amount is within the limit, you can likely use small claims. (If it doesn’t fit, or is explicitly excluded by law, we’ll flag that in the “cannot sue” section.)

### Contract & money-owed disputes

- **Breach of a written contract:** If someone fails to honor a written agreement and it costs you money, you can sue for those damages (up to $5k). *Examples:* You hired a contractor under a written contract to remodel a bathroom for $4,500, paid some or all of it, and the contractor didn’t do the work or did it defectively – you could sue to recover what you paid or the cost to fix. Or say you sold a used boat via a written installment contract, and the buyer stopped paying with $2,000 remaining – you can sue for the $2,000 balance. Louisiana gives a long time to bring contract cases (generally up to **10 years** for written contracts under the Civil Code). There is no requirement to send a demand letter or formal notice before suing for breach of contract (except in certain specific contracts or if the contract itself requires notice). However, it’s often wise to **make a written demand** first – both as a courtesy and because if the contract has an attorney-fee clause or you later seek court costs, showing that you tried to resolve it can only help. In small claims court, you should bring a **copy of the written contract** (or any written proof of the terms, like emails or invoices) as evidence of the agreement and the breach. Louisiana allows recovery of not just the out-of-pocket loss but also possibly any consequential damages from the breach (if provable and within the $5k cap). But you cannot ask the court to force the other party to *perform* the contract (specific performance) – you can only seek **money** to compensate your loss. 

- **Breach of an oral/handshake agreement:** Oral contracts are generally enforceable in Louisiana (except certain types that law requires to be written, like real estate sales). You can absolutely sue on a **verbal promise** or agreement – the challenge is proving its terms and that the other side agreed. In small claims, the judge will let you and your witnesses testify to what was said. *Examples:* You agreed to lend your neighbor your trailer in exchange for $300 and they promised to return it in good condition, but they damaged it – you could sue for the cost of repairs. Or you paid someone $200 to clean your yard and they never showed up – you can sue to get your $200 back. The key is convincing the judge that a specific agreement was made. It helps to have any **corroboration**: texts, emails, a witness who heard the conversation, or follow-up notes. The Louisiana Civil Code generally gives **10 years** to sue on oral contracts as well (treated as “personal actions” with a 10-year prescription unless covered by a special shorter period). One exception: if the debt is for **services or labor** you provided, that may be considered an open account (see below) with a shorter time. But for a one-time verbal agreement, you’re usually safe up to a few years. No special pre-suit notice is mandated, though again it’s good practice to remind the other party of their promise and give them a chance to fulfill it or pay you before you sue.

- **Unpaid loans or promissory notes:** Money you lent someone is a common small claim scenario. If you lent money on a **promissory note** (a signed IOU) or even just an informal loan, and haven’t been repaid, you can sue for the balance owed. *Example:* You loaned a friend $1,000 to fix their car, with a handwritten note that they’d repay within 6 months, and now it’s a year later with nothing paid – this is perfect for small claims. Or if you cosigned a loan and ended up paying it – you can sue the primary borrower for what you had to pay (since by law a cosigner who pays has the right to reimbursement). **Evidence:** Bring any documentation of the loan: the promissory note or IOU (even a text or email where they agree to pay you back $X). If the loan had specific terms (interest, due date), be prepared to show those. Louisiana law on promissory notes provides a **5-year** prescriptive period to sue from the due date of the note (notes are considered negotiable instruments with a 5-year statute of limitations)[29]. If there’s no set due date (a demand loan), it’s from the time you demanded repayment. If you charged interest and there was a written note, you can also claim the accrued interest (within the $5k cap). Also, Louisiana’s law on **acknowledgment** of debts: if the borrower made a partial payment or wrote to you admitting the debt, the time to sue might restart. No formal notice is required, but a **polite reminder/demand** in writing can often spur repayment or at least serve as evidence the debt was acknowledged. In small claims, you cannot recover **interest above legal rates** unless it was agreed in the note or provided by law – but you can get legal interest on the judgment (explained later in Post-judgment collection).

- **Unpaid invoices for goods or services (open accounts):** If you are a business (or an individual) owed money on an **open account** – for example, you sold goods or did work and billed the customer, and they haven’t paid – you can use small claims as long as the amount is $5k or less. In Louisiana, an open account typically means an ongoing credit relationship or multiple transactions not evidenced by a single note. *Examples:* A freelance graphic designer sent an invoice for $800 that the client never paid, or a small business delivered $2,500 in products to a customer on credit and hasn’t been paid. These are classic small claims. Louisiana has a helpful law: if a customer fails to pay an open-account debt within 30 days of **written demand correctly stating what’s owed**, the creditor is entitled by statute to **reasonable attorney’s fees** for the collection ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=,not%20exceed%20five%20thousand%20dollars)). This can apply in small claims – if you (as the plaintiff) followed the steps (sent a proper demand letter and waited 30 days), you can ask the judge for attorney’s fees. Even if you didn’t hire an attorney (as is common in small claims), you might still cite the law; some judges will award a modest amount for the time/effort of collection, though technically attorney-fee statutes are meant for actual attorney costs. Regardless, it’s good practice to send a **30-day demand letter by certified mail** for any significant unpaid invoice – it might prompt payment and, if not, it strengthens your case. Note: The prescriptive period for open account debts is **3 years** in Louisiana (shorter than the 10 for ordinary contracts) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=112111#:~:text=A,court%20is%20to%20be%20held)). This 3-year clock usually starts from the last charge or payment on the account. So, don’t delay too long in filing. In your small claim, provide the **invoice(s), purchase orders, delivery receipts, or account statements** showing the balance, and a copy of the demand letter you sent (and mail green card if returned).

- **Unpaid rent (residential or commercial):** A landlord can sue a former tenant in small claims for **unpaid rent** or other charges under the lease, as long as the claim is within $5,000. (Eviction for possession must be handled separately – see Landlord–Tenant section – but money claims for past rent or damages can be small claims.) *Examples:* Your tenant moved out owing 2 months’ rent at $800/month – you can sue for $1,600. Or a commercial tenant abandoned the space and left $5,000 in unpaid rent – you could sue for $5,000 here (if it were more, you’d go to a higher court). **Evidence:** Bring a copy of the **lease** (if written) and a **rent ledger or payment history** to show what was unpaid. Louisiana law views rent claims under a lease as either a personal action on the lease (10-year prescription) or sometimes as an open account (3-year) – but practically, you should file within 3 years to be safe ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=112111#:~:text=A,court%20is%20to%20be%20held)). No special demand is required by law like with deposits (though a **notice to vacate** is required before eviction, but that’s separate). If the tenant had a security deposit you have not returned, be careful: the law (see below) requires you to send an itemized list of deductions within 30 days of move-out. Landlords who wrongfully withhold deposits face penalties, so it’s wise to make sure you complied with that law before suing for additional rent or damages. If the deposit covered some of the owed rent, sue only for the remainder and explain the accounting to the judge. (You don’t want the tenant counter-suing for a deposit penalty in the same case.)

- **Bounced checks:** If someone paid you with a check that **bounced** (NSF – nonsufficient funds, or account closed) and they haven’t made the check good, you can sue for the amount of the check *plus* statutory penalties. Louisiana has a strict bad-check law: if the check writer doesn’t pay within 15 working days after you send them a written demand, you can recover **double the amount of the check (or $100, whichever is greater), plus court costs and reasonable attorney’s fees** . *Example:* A customer gave you a $500 check that bounced – you sent the proper demand letter and still weren’t paid. In court you can ask for $500 (the original amount) + $500 penalty + any mailing costs and possibly attorney fees. These penalties are capped to the court’s jurisdiction, so you can’t get more than $5,000 total in small claims (if the double amount would exceed $5k, you’d have to sue in regular court or waive the excess). **Procedure:** To invoke the penalties, you *must* send a **certified demand letter** to the check writer’s address shown on the check (or otherwise their last known address) giving them 15 working days to pay the amount of the check plus a service charge (Louisiana allows charging up to $25 or 5% as a service fee)[8]. The statute even provides specific wording for this notice  – it’s a good idea to use that or similarly explicit language. Bring a copy of the letter and the **certified mail receipt** (green card or tracking printout) showing it was delivered (or at least sent – under the law, the demand is considered made when sent to the address on the check, even if the recipient doesn’t accept it)[8]. Also bring the **returned check** (or bank printout of the NSF status). If the check writer offers to pay after you’ve filed, you might still be entitled to some costs or fees – often they’ll settle with paying the check plus your filing fee to avoid judgment. Note: You cannot both pursue criminal charges and get the civil penalty – but in small claims, we’re talking civil remedy. If you already got paid through the district attorney’s diversion program for bad checks, you can’t also recover in small claims. But if the DA wasn’t involved or the person didn’t go through that, you are free to sue civilly.

- **Open-book accounts / running tabs:** This is similar to the unpaid invoices scenario – an **open account** could be an ongoing tab at a store or for services (like a customer who kept accumulating credit). *Example:* A regular customer at your feed store bought feed on credit over months; now they owe $1,200 that they haven’t paid. Or a bar allowed a patron to run a tab of $300 and they walked out on it. These can be sued for in small claims. The same open-account rules (3-year prescription, 30-day demand for attorney fees) apply as described above for invoices. In practice, treat it the same: provide an **account statement** or ledger showing the purchases, credits, and balance. If finance charges were agreed on overdue balances, you can claim those up to the date of judgment (small claims judges might or might not award interest on the pre-judgment debt unless clearly agreed). Definitely send a **demand letter with an account audit** to the debtor – it not only helps with possible attorney fee recovery, it also might prompt them to settle or acknowledge the debt (which, if written, can interrupt prescription). 

- **Co-signer or guarantor reimbursement:** If you co-signed a loan or guaranteed someone’s obligation and ended up having to pay it yourself, you can sue the primary debtor for what *you* paid. Louisiana law (Civil Code art. 3045) gives a surety (co-signer) who pays the right to “step into the shoes” of the creditor. *Example:* You co-signed an apartment lease for a friend. The friend skipped out and the landlord made you pay $2,000 in back rent. You can sue your friend in small claims for $2,000. Provide proof you paid (receipts or a statement from the landlord showing you satisfied the debt) and a copy of the co-signed agreement if available. The prescriptive period would generally be the same as the original obligation (e.g. rent is 3 years or 10 years on written lease—either way, file as soon as possible). No special formalities—this is essentially an **indemnity** claim. It might be useful to send a demand or even have a written acknowledgement from the primary debtor that they’ll repay you (if you have texts or emails where they promise to “pay you back” for covering their debt, bring those). Often, co-signer cases are straightforward if you have the documents: the court isn’t fond of people welshing on co-signers who bailed them out.

### Consumer disputes

- **Refund for defective product:** If you bought a product (new or used) that turned out to be **defective or not as advertised**, and the seller refuses to refund or replace it, you can sue for a refund or for the cost of repair (whichever applies). *Examples:* You purchased a second-hand appliance for $600 and it died after one week; the seller wouldn’t give your money back – you can sue for the $600. Or you bought a new gadget that was advertised to do X but it doesn’t, and the store won’t take it back – sue for the price. Louisiana has a strong warranty law called **“redhibition”**: a seller warrants that the thing sold has no hidden defects that make it useless or very impaired in use. If it does, you as the buyer can seek to **rescind the sale** or reduce the price. In small claims, a judge *can* grant equitable relief like cancellation of the sale (essentially forcing a refund) since the statute permits “any appropriate relief” including equitable relief  , although typically you’ll just ask for your money back in lieu of returning the item. **State quirk:** If the seller knew of the defect and didn’t disclose it (a bad-faith seller), you could also get attorney fees in a redhibition suit – even in small claims, the court could award those if you prove the seller acted in bad faith. (For example, a used car dealer knowingly sold you a car with a cracked engine block.) But if the amount and evidence are small, you might just focus on the refund. *Pre-suit:* It’s very wise to **notify the seller** as soon as you discover the problem and give them a chance to fix it or refund you – not only because it might resolve the issue, but also because in redhibition, if the seller was in good faith, you must allow them an opportunity to repair. Sending a letter or email saying, “The item I bought on X date is defective (explain defect). I am requesting a refund of $Y or adequate repairs,” will satisfy this. Keep a copy. **Prescription:** For a good-faith seller, a redhibition action must be filed within **one year from the date you discovered the defect**, but at the latest within **two years of purchase** in any case . (For a bad-faith seller, you have one year from discovery with no absolute cutoff , and often the claim might also fall under fraud or LUTPA which have their own delays.) So don’t wait too long. Bring any **proof of purchase** (receipt, invoice) and any **expert report or repair estimate** if you have one (e.g. a mechanic’s statement that the engine was cracked). In small claims, expert testimony is rarely presented due to cost, but a written estimate or photos of the defect can help the judge see the problem.

- **Services not performed or done poorly:** If you paid for a service that was never delivered, or was done in a shoddy or incomplete way, you can sue to get your money back or the cost to have it redone. *Examples:* You paid a catering company a $500 deposit, but they never showed up at your event – you’d sue for the $500 plus any extra costs you incurred scrambling for replacement food. Or you hired a tree trimmer for $300 to removal limbs; they did half the job and quit – you could sue for the $150 of work not done (or the cost to hire someone else to finish). Another example: you paid a mechanic $400 to fix a specific issue, but they failed to fix it and refuse to refund – you can sue for the $400 (and perhaps any additional damage their failed repair caused, if within cap). These are simple breaches of the service contract. Louisiana does not have a special statute for poor services (aside from specific trades, e.g. home improvement contracting has licensing rules but not a separate small claims process). **Pre-suit:** It’s recommended to **give the provider a chance to make it right** – often a polite demand for a refund or fix will resolve it, especially if they fear a bad review or complaint. But if not, small claims is your remedy. When you sue, **document the issue**: any written contract or estimate, proof of payment, and evidence of the poor work (photos of the unfinished or substandard work, or a report from another professional who fixed it). If you had to pay someone else to redo the job, that cost is your damage (as long as it’s reasonable and you mitigated losses). Louisiana’s Unfair Trade Practices Act (LUTPA) (see below) can sometimes apply if the conduct was egregiously deceptive (e.g. the provider actively misled you), potentially allowing attorney fees or even treble damages for willful fraud ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77969#:~:text=Committees%20,Input)) – but in small claims, judges may be reluctant to label something a LUTPA violation unless it’s clearly more than a breach. Usually, you’ll proceed as a straightforward breach of contract or **negligence** claim. The prescriptive period is generally **10 years** for written service contracts, **10 years** for oral (unless categorized as wages or open account which are 3 years) – but practically, file within 1–3 years while evidence is fresh.

- **Consumer product warranty issues:** If a product was sold with a **warranty (written or implied)** and it fails and the seller/manufacturer won’t honor the warranty, you can sue for the cost of repair or replacement. *Example:* You bought a new appliance with a 1-year warranty, it broke in 6 months and the manufacturer’s authorized repair never fixes it, and they refuse to replace – you could sue for the refund or replacement value (depreciated maybe). Often, these disputes involve manufacturer warranties, which can be a bit complex (sometimes federal warranty law – Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act – applies, which *does* allow small claims actions for breach of warranty). In small claims, you’d typically sue the **seller** if the manufacturer is out of state (since getting jurisdiction over an out-of-state manufacturer might be tricky unless they have a presence in Louisiana). Louisiana’s redhibition law also covers serious breaches of warranty (as an alternative to Magnuson-Moss). *State quirk:* Redhibition effectively provides an implied warranty on used items sold by any seller (if sold “as is,” the warranty might be waived for obvious defects, but not for intentional concealment). So even without an official warranty, you often have rights. For small claims, focus on the money: either the cost to fix the item or the amount you paid for it if it’s unusable. Bring any **warranty documents** and **records of repair attempts**. Also, show you followed any required steps (like notifying the warranty provider). If the issue falls under the federal Magnuson-Moss Act, you could also recover attorney fees if you win – though again, in small claims with no attorneys, that’s mostly moot. Prescriptive period: treat it as redhibition (1 year from discovery) or breach of contract (10 years) depending on scenario – either way, file as soon as it’s clear they won’t honor the warranty.

- **No-show or canceled services (event vendors, etc.):** If you paid someone for a service tied to a specific event or date (wedding vendor, band, photographer, caterer, venue, etc.) and they canceled last-minute or never showed up, you can sue to get your money back and any extra costs you incurred. *Example:* You reserved an event hall for $1,000 and the hall double-booked your date and canceled your booking, refusing to refund the deposit – you can sue for your $1,000 plus perhaps any additional expense you incurred to find a new venue on short notice (if within the cap). As another example, a wedding photographer took a $800 deposit but then didn’t show or never delivered photos – you’d sue for the $800 and any demonstrable losses from not having those services. These cases often involve a **breach of contract** (written or oral). If you had a written contract with cancellation terms, those will be important: many venue or vendor contracts have clauses about non-refundable deposits or liquidated damages. Judges will consider those, though if the vendor clearly breached (as in they canceled on you), usually the contract won’t allow them to keep money for services not provided (unless the contract explicitly made the deposit non-refundable under any circumstances, which some do – even then, a judge might find keeping it is unjust if they voluntarily canceled). **Evidence:** Provide the contract, any emails/texts about the cancellation, and proof of what you paid. Also show that you attempted to mitigate damages (e.g. you found an alternative service, albeit maybe at higher cost, which you can claim). Louisiana doesn’t have a special statute for event cancellations, so it’s basic contract law. There’s no required notice or anything – the breach itself is your cause. If multiple customers were affected (like a venue that shut down and kept lots of deposits), each can file in small claims separately (not a class action). The prescriptive period is 10 years for written contracts. One note: If you received some benefit before the cancellation (say a planner did part of the work), the judge might not award a full refund of a deposit–they might subtract the value of what was done. But if it was a total breach or no value given (hall gave you nothing), you get all your money back. Occasionally, such scenarios can overlap with Louisiana’s **Unfair Trade Practices Act** if the business was deceptive or acted in bad faith (for instance, taking deposits while planning to close business). LUTPA suits can potentially allow **treble damages and attorney fees** for knowing fraud ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77969#:~:text=Committees%20,Input)), but invoking LUTPA in small claims is tricky – you’d need to prove the behavior was egregiously wrongful and falls under the statute (which is often done in regular court for larger cases). Still, you can mention it if applicable (the small claims judge can consider awarding at least attorney fees under LUTPA if you technically satisfy it, though treble damages might push you over the cap or not be entertained in this forum).

- **Dry cleaning / property damage in custody:** If a business to which you entrusted your property damages or loses it – for example, a dry cleaner ruined your clothes, a hotel lost your luggage, a valet damaged your car, or a storage facility allowed your items to be destroyed – you can sue for the value of the property or the cost of repair. *Examples:* A dry cleaner shrunk and tore a custom dress valued $500 and only offers $50 – you can sue for the full value (assuming $500 < $5k). A parking valet returned your car with a dent and refuses to pay for repairs – if the repair is $300, that’s a valid small claim. Louisiana law often deems these situations a **bailment**, where the custodian (bailee) has a duty of reasonable care. If they fail and your property is damaged, they are liable for the damage caused by their negligence. No special statute compels pre-suit action, but definitely **make a claim or complaint** with the business first – many have internal processes or insurance to cover such mishaps. If they deny or lowball you, gather evidence: photos of the damage, receipts or appraisals for the item’s value, and any relevant tickets or contracts (e.g. the claim check from the dry cleaner or valet stub – note some have disclaimer fine print like “not responsible for damage,” but such blanket disclaimers don’t always shield them from gross negligence). In small claims, the judge will evaluate what’s a fair value of the loss – typically either the **market value** of the item (if destroyed) or the **cost to repair** (if partially damaged). Keep in mind, for used items like clothing, you usually can’t get the full original price if it had substantial wear – the court might depreciate. However, if it was brand new or had special value and the cleaner accepted it knowing that, you can argue for full value. Louisiana doesn’t have a specific consumer law forcing triple damages or anything for these cases (except if it’s willful maybe LUTPA could apply, but that’s rare here). It’s mainly a negligence claim with a one-year prescriptive period (damage to movable property is generally treated as a tort, so **1 year** from the incident to sue)[22]. So do act within a year. 

- **Gym memberships / subscription services – refunds or cancellations:** If you canceled a service like a gym membership, cable/Internet, alarm service, or any recurring subscription, and the company continues to charge you or refuses a refund of prepaid amounts, you can sue for the money you believe you’re owed. *Examples:* You paid upfront for a one-year gym membership ($600) and after 3 months you properly canceled under the contract due to relocation, but the gym won’t refund the unused 9 months – you could sue for that prorated amount. Or you followed the procedure to cancel your streaming service, but your credit card keeps getting charged $50/month for several months – you can claim those charges back. Louisiana, like many states, has an “Automatic Renewal” statute that requires clear disclosure of auto-renewal terms and easy cancellation (La. R.S. 9:2716); if a company violated that (for instance, never told you it would auto-renew or didn’t get your affirmative consent), that law gives you rights to cancel and stop future charges (and possibly get a refund). Enforcement of that is usually through the Attorney General, but you can use it in your argument to bolster that the continued charges are unlawful. When suing, **show proof of your cancellation**: copy of the letter or email you sent, the confirmation number of phone cancellation, etc. Also show the charges that hit afterward (bank statements, credit card bills). Many large companies have arbitration clauses that cover consumer disputes – however, notably, some contracts *exclude small claims court* from the arbitration requirement, meaning they allow you to use small claims. Check your membership contract terms: if it says you can go to small claims for disputes, you’re good. If it has a strict arbitration clause with no exclusion, the company might try to move to arbitration – but often for the small amounts, they won’t bother and might just pay once you file. Louisiana’s Unfair Trade Practices law could be cited if there was deceptive conduct (e.g. gym lied about cancellation policy), which might allow attorney fees, but realistically if you prove you canceled and they kept charging, the judge will refund those extra charges as straightforward unjust enrichment. The prescriptive period for a claim like this (breach of a service contract or unfair trade practice) could be argued as 10 years (contract) or 1 year (unfair trade practice). To be safe, file within 1 year of the issue. 

- **Auto repair disputes (poor work or overcharging):** If you had your vehicle repaired or serviced and believe the mechanic either **charged for work not done** or **did a bad job** that caused you loss, you can use small claims (if within $5k). *Examples:* You paid a mechanic $800 to replace your car’s timing belt and water pump, but later discovered they never replaced the belt at all (and your engine was damaged as a result) – you could sue for the $800 and related damages (maybe the subsequent repair costs, if not too high). Or a body shop charged you for OEM parts but used cheaper aftermarket parts without telling you, or left some damage unrepaired – you could claim the difference in value or cost to fix properly. **Evidence:** These cases benefit greatly from an **expert evaluation** – e.g., another mechanic or shop that looked at the car and can provide a written statement of what was or wasn’t done right. In small claims, you likely won’t have that person testify (unless they’ll come for free), but a written report or estimate can carry weight (the judge may or may not accept it fully, but since evidence rules are relaxed, hearsay like a written statement can be considered if deemed reliable[5]). Also bring the **invoice/receipt** from the original repair and any photos (for body work issues). If you were overcharged, show what the fair price should have been (maybe an estimate from elsewhere). Louisiana doesn’t have a specific “Auto Repair Act” providing statutory damages – it’s contract and fraud law. If a shop committed fraud (e.g. charged for new parts they didn’t install), that’s also potentially a **criminal offense** (theft/fraud) and a LUTPA violation. You could mention LUTPA for such egregious behavior – under LUTPA, you could get attorney fees and possibly treble damages if the conduct was knowingly deceptive and flagged by authorities ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77969#:~:text=Committees%20,Input)). Even if you don’t formally invoke that law, judges dislike dishonest mechanics and may lean toward full reimbursement. No special pre-suit step is required, but giving the shop a chance to fix the mistake (if you trust them at all) is wise. Often, though, by the time someone comes to small claims, trust has eroded. The prescriptive period on a claim for damage to your car from negligent repair is likely **1 year** (tort), or if viewed as breach of the service contract, maybe longer – but assume 1 year from when you discovered the bad work and try to sue within that.

- **Home improvement contractor disputes:** (This overlaps with “services” and “property damage,” but it’s common enough to highlight.) If you hired a contractor or handyman for repairs or remodeling on your home and they **didn’t complete the work or did it improperly**, you can use small claims for the financial loss – bearing in mind that many home projects exceed $5k in value, but smaller jobs qualify. *Examples:* You paid a handyman $1,200 to repaint several rooms and install some fixtures; they did half then disappeared – you could sue for the value of the unfinished work (say $600) plus any costs to fix mistakes they made. Or a fence installer put up a fence with the wrong materials or large gaps, essentially breaching the contract – if you paid $4,000 and it’s unsalvageable, you might sue for the cost to re-do (assuming it doesn’t exceed $5k). **Louisiana specific:** Residential contractors doing home improvement over $7,500 are supposed to be licensed – if you hired an unlicensed person for a big job, they technically shouldn’t be doing that job. While their unlicensed status doesn’t prevent you from suing (indeed, it can only help your case to show they weren’t licensed if they did poor work), an unlicensed contractor also cannot file a lien against your property (so no fear of that in small claims context). **Evidence:** Have the **contract** or written estimate if any. Document what was done versus what wasn’t (photos of incomplete areas, etc.). If you brought in someone else to finish or fix, get a **written estimate or invoice** from them to show the cost difference. There’s also a Louisiana New Home Warranty Act and a licensing board for bigger cases, but for minor renovations those typically don’t come into play. It’s primarily a breach of contract. As with other services, try to communicate with the contractor first – sometimes they disappear due to personal issues and might come back if prompted; if not, a demand letter sets the stage. This type of claim prescribes in **10 years** if viewed as breach of a building contract (written), or 1 year if viewed as tort for property damage – since the work relates to immovable property (your house), a claim for defective work could be considered a **construction defect** which has a special peremptive period of 5 years for major structural issues by statute. But for a simple contract breach, you have time – still, small claims should be filed as soon as it’s clear the contractor won’t make it right.

- **Auto sales (used car problems, etc.):** Disputes over car purchases often exceed small claims limits, but if yours doesn’t (e.g. a cheap used car) or you are willing to settle for $5k, you can use small claims. *Examples:* You bought a used car for $4,000 from a local seller “as is,” and the next day the transmission fails – if you can prove the seller knew of the issue, you could sue for redhibition (return the car for a refund). Or the seller lied about the car’s history (rolled back odometer or salvage title not disclosed) – you can sue for the diminished value or cancel the sale. Louisiana’s **redhibition law** applies to car sales too: even “as is” sales don’t protect a seller who **fraudulently conceals** defects. Louisiana also has a “Lemon Law” for new vehicles, but those claims (full refund or replacement of a new car) typically are above $5k and handled in other courts or arbitration. However, small claims can handle certain aspects – for instance, if a **used car dealer** sold you a car with a warranty and isn’t honoring it, you can claim the cost of repairs under that warranty (if under $5k). Or if the car had a defect that should have been covered by Louisiana’s **Used Car Warranty Act** (Louisiana doesn’t have a strong used car warranty statute like some states, but dealers cannot misrepresent a vehicle). If there was **odometer fraud**, federal law allows a suit for treble damages or $10,000 minimum – but again, that exceeds small claims; you could instead sue under state law for the actual difference in value and mention the fraud. If a **title** wasn’t provided, you could sue the seller for rescission (because you can’t legally use the car without title). *Pre-suit:* Notify the seller or dealer immediately upon discovering an issue and give them a chance to fix it or discuss. Car dealers often have you sign arbitration agreements – check that. If the dealer is part of a chain with an arbitration clause, they may move to compel arbitration, but many smaller dealers don’t or won’t pursue that for $4k. A private seller likely no arbitration to worry about. **Evidence:** If alleging the seller knew of a defect, you need some proof – maybe the mechanic says the problem clearly pre-dated the sale, or the seller’s ad claimed “new transmission” which was false. Keep any texts with the seller. If it’s a pure redhibition claim (defect makes car unusable), the court may require you to tender the car back upon refund – essentially undoing the sale. That’s fine in small claims – just be prepared to hand over the vehicle if you win a full refund (or whatever the judge orders). These cases can be complex, but small claims can resolve simpler scenarios where the numbers are small. *Prescription:* Redhibition against a good-faith seller is 1 year from discovery (or max 1 year from purchase for used personal vehicles sold between individuals, per some jurisprudence) . Against a bad-faith seller, 1 year from discovery with no cap . Fraud claims are 1 year from discovery. So timeline is tight – act quickly when you realize you were duped or the car is a dud.

- **False advertising or unfair trade practices:** If a business misled you or acted fraudulently, causing you financial loss under $5,000, you can bring a claim that essentially falls under Louisiana’s Unfair Trade Practices and Consumer Protection Law (UTPCPL). *Examples:* A store advertised a laptop as including certain features which it did not; you relied on that and spent $1,000 on it – you could sue for a refund due to the false advertising. Or a contractor used deceptive sales tactics to get you to sign up for a $3,000 service you didn’t need – you can sue for your money back. Louisiana’s UTPCPL (R.S. 51:1401 et seq.) prohibits unfair or deceptive acts in commerce. It allows a private lawsuit for **actual damages**, and if the conduct was willful and the Attorney General had put the company on notice that it was unlawful, the court *must* award **treble damages** and can award attorney’s fees ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77969#:~:text=Committees%20,Input)). In most small claims scenarios, you won’t have that AG notice aspect – but you can still sue for your actual loss and ask the court to also award you reasonable attorney’s fees under the law (and court costs) if you prove the practice was unfair or deceptive. *Note:* UTPCPL claims have a **one-year prescriptive period** (short – 1 year from the transaction or when you discovered the unfair practice). Also, some transactions (like between individuals, or isolated sales) might not be covered by the act – it primarily covers business-to-consumer commerce. In small claims, a judge may not be intimately familiar with UTPCPL but will understand straightforward fraud. So even if you don’t label it UTPCPL, you can argue you were defrauded and seek your money. If you do explicitly invoke UTPCPL, do so only if the situation genuinely involves egregious fraud or clearly unethical behavior – not just a breach of contract. And know that **punitive damages** (like the trebling) won’t be awarded absent the specific circumstances (AG notice) and would push beyond $5k anyway. Focus on getting your actual loss back. If you win under UTPCPL, you are entitled to attorney fees – which in small claims might be nominal or just your court costs if you didn’t have an attorney. Still, the threat of UTPCPL can be a good pressure in settlement negotiations.

### Property damage

- **Vehicle accidents (minor collisions):** If someone damages your vehicle in a traffic accident and your property damage claim is within $5,000, you can sue the at-fault driver in small claims for the cost of repairs, deductible, rental car, etc. (Personal injury claims from accidents often exceed $5k and belong in regular court – see “Personal injury” below – but many property-only claims are small enough for small claims). *Example:* A driver rear-ended your car causing $2,800 in damage and they won’t pay – you can sue for $2,800 (plus perhaps towing and a few days’ rental car). **Evidence:** Provide a **repair estimate or bill** from a body shop, and photographs of the damage if available. Also bring any **police report** or exchange-of-information from the accident to help establish fault (police reports aren’t automatically evidence of fault, but in small claims, a judge may give weight to an officer’s finding or citation issued). Louisiana is a comparative fault state – if the other driver or their insurance claimed you were partly at fault and that’s why they didn’t pay, the small claims judge can assign percentages of fault. For instance, if you were 20% at fault, the judge might award you only 80% of your damages. Be prepared to argue why the other party was 100% at fault. If you have **insurance**, usually your own insurer will handle this kind of claim (either through collision coverage or subrogation against the other driver). But maybe your deductible or an uncovered loss remains – you can claim that portion. Or if the other driver was uninsured and you didn’t have collision, small claims is the route to recover. Louisiana’s “No Pay, No Play” law says an uninsured driver cannot collect the first $25,000 of property damages from an insured at-fault driver. So if *you* were uninsured, unfortunately you generally *cannot recover* your car damage (since it’s below $25k, it’s essentially barred). Small claims judges will follow that law if raised (it’s R.S. 32:866). If both parties were insured or that law doesn’t apply, proceed as normal. Also, Louisiana’s Direct Action statute allows you to directly sue the other driver’s **insurance company** within the same small claim[19]. In practice, you could name both the driver and their insurer as defendants in your small claim. Often the insurer will then handle the defense and might remove the case to a higher court if they want – but for amounts this small, they might simply show up in small claims or settle. Note that if the other driver’s insurer already offered you something (e.g. partial payment) and you refused, small claims might encourage a middle-ground settlement. Always sue for the **full amount of your loss** (e.g. full repair cost, rental car fees, and any **diminished value** if applicable (within cap)), and let the judge decide if something should be subtracted. One-year prescriptive period applies to car accident damage claims in Louisiana (they are delictual actions)[22]. So you must file within one year of the accident. Lawsuits against out-of-state drivers can be served through the Louisiana long-arm statute or the Secretary of State (no extra fee for serving through the Sec. of State in small claims cases)[27]. If you win and the other driver doesn’t pay voluntarily, you can use collection methods discussed later (and if they still don’t pay, you can also send proof of the judgment to the OMV – non-payment of a property damage judgment can lead to license suspension for the at-fault driver).

- **Damage to personal property by someone:** Any situation where a person’s negligence (or intentional act) **damaged your personal property** can be the basis of a small claim, if the amount is $5k or less. *Examples:* A guest in your house accidentally knocked over an antique vase worth $600 and won’t compensate you – you can sue them for $600. Or a friend borrowed your power tools and returned them broken, refusing to repair or replace – if the tools cost $300, that’s eligible. Or your neighbor, while repairing their fence, dropped a plank on your lawnmower and ruined it – you could claim the mower’s value. These are typically **tort claims** (specifically property damage due to negligence) and have a **prescription of 1 year** from the date of damage[22]. If it was intentional damage (vandalism), it’s still a tort (intentional tort) with one-year limit, and you could also pursue criminal charges separately, but the small claim would be your civil remedy for money. In small claims, you must prove the **amount of your loss** – bring receipts or evidence of the item’s value (if you don’t have the purchase receipt, you can get quotes of its current value or repair estimates). If the item was beyond repair, usually you claim its **fair market value** (what a willing buyer would pay for it in its condition just before damage). If it’s a relatively new item, an original purchase receipt (even if a year old) can serve as evidence of value. If it had specific value (like a collectible), maybe bring an appraisal or online listing of similar items. Also, prove the **responsibility**: photos of the aftermath, eyewitness statements (even your own testimony, or a friend who saw it happen). The defendant might have a defense like “it was an accident, I said sorry” – but apologies don’t pay bills; in law, if they were negligent, they owe you. Louisiana doesn’t impose punitive damages for simple property damage, so you only sue for actual damages plus court costs. One special case: if a **minor** caused the damage (say a child broke your window), you should generally sue the minor’s parent or legal guardian – Louisiana law (Civil Code art. 2318) makes parents liable for damage by their unemancipated minors. So list the parent as defendant, referencing the child’s action. If a **pet** caused the damage (e.g. neighbor’s dog chewed your expensive lawn chair), that’s the owner’s liability (see next bullet on pet damage). Prepare to show the value and the circumstances, and small claims should handle it fine.

- **Pet-related damage:** If someone’s **animal** caused damage to your property or your pet, you can use small claims. *Examples:* Your neighbor’s dog got into your yard and injured your dog, resulting in $800 in vet bills – you can claim those vet expenses. Or your friend’s large dog knocked over your TV causing $400 damage – you can claim the $400. Another scenario: your dog was attacked by another dog and you had to pay vet costs – you can sue the other owner. Louisiana law holds pet owners responsible for damage caused by their animals if the owner was **negligent** in failing to prevent it (or strictly liable if they knew or should have known of the animal’s dangerous tendencies). For a simple situation like property damage or vet bills, you don’t have to delve deep into the statutes – you just show their pet caused the harm. *Evidence:* Photos of your injured pet or damaged property, **vet invoices** or repair bills, and perhaps witness testimony if the incident was disputed (“I saw their dog bite my dog” or “neighbors will testify the dog roams free”). If two pets fought, small claims won’t determine “doggy fault” – it will focus on owner fault. Typically, if your pet was on a leash or on your property and another wasn’t, the other owner is at fault. If both were loose in a dog park, recovery might be harder absent clear aggression by one. Pet injury/death claims in Louisiana legally treat pets as **property**, so you can recover vet bills and perhaps some “sentimental value” if argued, but not huge emotional distress damages. (Small claims judges may empathize and could award a token amount for distress, but legally it’s the economic loss that’s compensable – vet costs, market value of pet if deceased, etc.) The prescriptive period is **1 year** for damage or injury caused by an animal (it’s a delictual action)[22]. Also note, if a **dog bite** caused injury to a person (like medical bills for a person), that often exceeds $5k (medical + possibly pain and suffering) and typically goes to regular court (and often involves homeowners insurance). But for smaller bites or just property/pet damage, small claims works. Many times, the pet owner’s **homeowners insurance** will cover pet-caused damages – it’s worth asking the defendant beforehand if they’ll make a claim; if they do and insurance pays, you might avoid court. If not, bring it to court. Louisiana also has a statute making dog owners strictly liable for injuries to people (Civil Code art. 2321) except if victim provoked – but again, small claims likely won’t hinge on the legal nuance as much as the straightforward facts of negligence (e.g., did they let their dog run loose in violation of leash laws?). 

- **Damage by tenants or landlords:** If a *tenant* caused damage to a rental property or if a *landlord* caused damage to a tenant’s belongings, those are actionable in small claims (financial only, not eviction or such). For landlord claims against tenants: you can sue a former tenant for the cost to repair damage beyond normal wear-and-tear (see Landlord–Tenant section for deposit rules and penalties – make sure you handled the deposit properly first). For tenant claims against landlords: if your landlord’s negligence caused damage to your personal property (say, they failed to fix a leak and your furniture was ruined, or during renovations the landlord dropped something on your TV), you can sue for the value of your items. Landlords are generally responsible for maintaining the premises (Civil Code art. 2697) and could be liable for property losses if they knew of a serious issue and didn’t fix it or directly caused the damage. These cases essentially follow the same principles as any property damage case, but often intersect with **security deposit** issues or **lease duties**. If you’re a tenant suing a landlord for property loss, be prepared for the landlord to argue you could have moved your items or that they weren’t negligent – you’ll have to show the landlord had a duty and breached it (e.g., ignored a known roof leak). If you’re a landlord suing a tenant for property damage, expect the tenant to possibly counterclaim for deposit penalties if you didn’t follow deposit law (and note that deposit penalties can be *two times* the deposit or $300, whichever greater ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=1238489#:~:text=Louisiana%20Laws%20,amount%20over%20the%20jurisdiction%20of))). So landlords, always send the deposit accounting within 30 days and only charge reasonable, actual damages. Small claims judges see many deposit and damage disputes and will try to be fair. They often offset deposit penalties against claimed damages if both are at issue. It’s wise for each side to document the condition of the rental (photos at move-in and move-out). If you have those, bring them. The prescriptive period on property damage to a rental property (landlord’s claim) is likely 3 years (it’s pursuant to the lease duties) or arguably 1 year (if treated as tort – but since it’s related to a lease, many would say the action is contractual with 3-year period for waste). For tenant claims against landlord, it could be 1 year as well (tort for negligence). Safer to assume **1 year** from move-out or incident for any of these to avoid prescription issues, since many court opinions on lease damage are under tort concepts.

### Employment & wages

*(Small claims can be a faster way for workers to recover money they’re owed. However, note that certain wage claims might also be handled by the Louisiana Workforce Commission or federal Department of Labor. Still, nothing stops you from using small claims for straightforward wage disputes as long as you’re not seeking statutory penalties that exceed $5k.)*

- **Unpaid wages or final paycheck:** Louisiana law requires employers to pay employees promptly after discharge or resignation – no later than the next regular payday or 15 days, whichever comes first ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77964#:~:text=long%20as%20the%20action%20remains,of%20process%20is%20required%20to)) ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77964#:~:text=Additionally%2C%20the%20court%20may%20require,in%20a%20small%20claims%20division)). If an employer fails to pay owed wages without a good-faith dispute, the employee can recover not just the wages but also a penalty of up to **90 days’ wages** (at the employee’s daily rate) and **attorney’s fees** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=This%20media,not%20necessarily%20the%20official%20citation)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=of%20substantive%20law%2C%20and%20shall,provisions%20relating%20to%20privileged%20communications)). This is often called “waiting time penalties.” Small claims court is a common venue for workers to claim their last paycheck or other earned wages under this law (La. R.S. 23:631 and 23:632). *Examples:* Your former employer didn’t pay your last week’s pay of $500 – you can sue for that $500. If 30 days have passed since you demanded it, you can also ask for the penalty – e.g. if you earned $100/day, 90 days’ penalty would be $9,000, but you’d be limited to $5,000 of that in small claims (you might choose to waive the excess and claim $5,000).
