Or say your employer failed to pay you a $300 bonus that was in your contract when you left – that’s owed wages too. **Procedure:** Before suing for wages, the law requires that you **“make demand”** on the employer after termination and wait at least 3 days ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=of%20substantive%20law%2C%20and%20shall,provisions%20relating%20to%20privileged%20communications)). In practice, this is usually done by a written demand (e.g. a letter or even an email to HR or the boss saying “I haven’t received my final paycheck for [dates]; please provide it”). Do this as soon as you realize your check isn’t coming. Keep proof of the demand (certified mail receipt, or a copy of email). If the employer has a **good-faith dispute** (e.g. you were paid all wages but you think otherwise, or a genuine disagreement on amount), a court can deny the penalty – but if they clearly just didn’t pay with no valid reason, the penalty is usually awarded. In small claims, you’d sue for the amount of wages owed and explicitly cite **La. R.S. 23:632** for penalty wages and attorney fees. The judge will often award the wages plus court costs. Penalty wages up to 90 days’ worth can be awarded at the judge’s discretion (the statute says “shall be liable for 90 days’ wages *or* full wages from demand until payment, whichever is less” – so maximum 90 days) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=This%20media,not%20necessarily%20the%20official%20citation)). If your claim plus penalties exceed $5k, you might have to waive some or sue in a higher court – many times, though, unpaid wage claims are small enough to fit even with penalties. Also note, if you win even a dollar of unpaid wages, the statute says the court **shall award you reasonable attorney’s fee** ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=of%20substantive%20law%2C%20and%20shall,provisions%20relating%20to%20privileged%20communications)) (in small claims, again, you likely have no attorney, but you could ask for some small amount for your time or any consultation you did). The prescriptive period for unpaid wage claims under this statute is **3 years** (treated as an action for unpaid compensation)[28]. However, the penalty portion might be considered a **penalty** that prescribes in 1 year by some courts – but generally, courts allow three years for the whole claim since it’s tied to wages. It’s best not to delay – wages are your livelihood, so small claims judges expect you to pursue them promptly. 

- **Unpaid overtime, commissions, or bonuses:** If your employer failed to pay earned **overtime** or promised commissions/bonuses, you may bring that to small claims as a breach of wage law or contract. *Examples:* You earned $500 in sales commissions that your employer never paid after you quit – that’s recoverable. Or you consistently worked overtime hours but the company didn’t pay overtime (and you’re a non-exempt employee entitled to it) – you can claim the unpaid overtime (1.5× rate for hours over 40 per week under FLSA). Note that **overtime claims** could also be taken to federal court under the Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA), which allows double damages and attorney fees. But the FLSA doesn’t bar you from suing in state court for the owed wages themselves. In small claims, you could claim just the base unpaid overtime (or base + the FLSA liquidated damages if you explicitly invoke FLSA; but adding liquidated double damages might push over $5k or complicate things). You might keep it simple and sue for the unpaid hours at the overtime rate. *Commissions/bonuses:* If you had a clear agreement that you’d be paid a certain commission or performance bonus and it was earned, it’s considered wages. Louisiana courts have treated certain unpaid commissions as wages subject to the same final paycheck law (if they were measurable and due at termination). However, truly discretionary bonuses (like a gift or purely at employer’s will) are not enforceable. Provide any **written plan or emails** that spell out the commission/bonus entitlement. If you met the targets, show that (sales records, etc.). Employers sometimes withhold commissions if a customer hasn’t paid – but unless your commission agreement says **“paid when we get paid”**, you likely can demand it after a reasonable time. For overtime, you should bring evidence of the hours you worked each week (timesheets, punch records, or even personal logs if that’s all you have). The burden can be on the employer to disprove if you show enough evidence you did overtime. If your claim is significant (and especially if other employees are also affected), consider contacting the U.S. Dept. of Labor or LA Workforce Commission – but those processes can take time, and you won’t get penalty damages through them usually. In small claims, you can at least get your wages and possibly statutory penalties (FLSA gives liquidated damages equal to the unpaid overtime, essentially doubling it, if the employer can’t prove a good-faith reason for underpaying). State law doesn’t have separate overtime penalties aside from the same final wage penalty if it applies. The prescriptive period for unpaid **wage claims (including commissions)** is **3 years** in Louisiana ([legis.la.gov](https://legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=112111#:~:text=A,court%20is%20to%20be%20held)). For **overtime under FLSA**, technically it’s 2 years (or 3 if willful violation) under federal law – but if you sue under state law for the same wages, it might be 3. Safer to use 2 years for overtime claims to avoid issues. 

- **Business expense reimbursement:** If your employer agreed to reimburse you for work-related expenses (mileage, purchases, travel costs) and didn’t, you can sue for those amounts. *Example:* You spent $300 on supplies for a project (with your boss’s approval) and your employer never paid you back. Or you traveled for work, charged $800 to your personal card for hotels, and the company refuses to reimburse after you quit. These are essentially breach of agreement claims. Louisiana wage law doesn’t treat expenses exactly as “wages” (so the 90-day penalty may not apply to unpaid reimbursements), but if it was promised, it’s a debt you can pursue. Show the **receipts** and any policy or emails showing they were to be reimbursed. Often, companies have expense reimbursement policies; if you followed them and got stiffed, that’s in your favor. If the amount is small, sometimes a call or letter to HR resolves it – they might have “fallen through the cracks.” If not, small claims will handle it as a straight debt. The prescriptive period could be 3 years (if it’s considered compensation due) or 10 years (written contract) – unclear, but certainly within 3 years you should file. 

- **Misclassification (contractor vs employee) claims:** If you were treated as an independent contractor but believe you were an employee and thus entitled to certain wages or overtime, that can be complex – often handled by agencies (LWC or IRS) – but small claims can handle the core money dispute if it’s straightforward. For example, if you can show you should have been paid overtime or minimum wage and you weren’t, you can claim those amounts. Do note, however, that determining employee status can be legally intricate, and an employer might push back hard or remove to a higher court. If the amount is small and the facts clearly show you were essentially an employee (they controlled your schedule, etc.), a small claims judge might be willing to rule on it. But be prepared to prove the “economic reality” of your role as an employee. Sometimes it’s easier to file a wage claim with the state or federal labor department in such cases. In small claims, you *cannot* get a ruling that changes your status for tax or benefits purposes – only money. So focus on the money aspect (e.g., “I worked 50 hours/week at $10/hr with no overtime – I’m owed overtime pay for 10 hours per week”). You’d use FLSA standards (which the judge can apply; state courts have concurrent jurisdiction for FLSA claims). The prescriptive period for overtime/minimum wage under FLSA is 2 years (3 if willful). So don’t wait. Also note, some wage claims are outside small claims: e.g. claims for **wrongful termination, discrimination, or unpaid insurance benefits** – those go through other channels (EEOC, etc.) or higher courts, not small claims. Small claims is just for straightforward pay/benefits that can be seen as a debt. If you’re unsure, consult LWC or an employment attorney.

*(Note: The Louisiana Workforce Commission can handle some wage claims (especially unpaid final wages or minimum wage/overtime) through its administrative process. That can be free and doesn’t require you to go to court. However, the LWC cannot award penalty wages (only the court can) and its process may be slower or less certain of recovery. Many workers use small claims because it puts pressure on the employer to respond quickly, and the court can garnish or seize assets if the judgment isn’t paid. There’s no rule forcing you to go through LWC first – it’s an alternative route.)*

### Personal services / professional disputes

- **Personal care services (hair, nails, etc.):** If you paid for a personal service like a haircut, hair coloring, manicure, etc., and it was botched – resulting in you spending more money to fix it or suffering loss – you can sue the provider for that cost. *Examples:* You paid $250 for a hair treatment that went awry (hair was damaged), and you had to pay another salon $200 to correct it – you could sue to recover the $200 corrective cost (and possibly some of the original $250 if the result was worthless). Or a nail salon’s technician injured your nail bed, causing medical treatment – you could claim the doctor bill and maybe a refund. In these cases, you’re essentially claiming the service was performed **negligently or not as agreed**. Louisiana’s general rules apply: if a service provider doesn’t use ordinary skill and care and causes damage, they’re liable for the resulting loss. There’s no special “Hairdresser Liability Act,” so it’s just a regular claim. Because these often involve some personal element (e.g. hair damage, embarrassment), you might wonder about pain & suffering. For very minor injuries or cosmetic issues, judges might not award much if anything for emotional distress – they’ll focus on out-of-pocket costs (like costs of repair, medical bills for a scalp injury, etc.). You likely won’t get punitive damages for a bad haircut (punitive only if statute allows, which it doesn’t here). **Evidence:** Photos of the bad outcome (before and after, if available), any **expert letter** from another stylist stating what was done wrong (if you obtained one), and your receipts from fixing the issue. If you experienced physical injury (burns from a chemical, infection from unsanitary tools), get medical records or doctor’s note. Also, a **written statement from the salon** (if they apologized or admitted fault in a text or Yelp response) can help. Pre-suit, it’s definitely worth asking the salon/individual for a refund or remedy – many will offer a free fix or refund if they clearly messed up, to avoid bad publicity or legal trouble. If they don’t and your damages are within $5k, small claims is viable. Prescriptive period: likely **1 year** from the service (since it’s essentially a tort of negligent service causing damage). If it’s framed as breach of contract (“I asked for blonde highlights and got green hair”), one could argue 10-year, but the damage to hair or body is essentially a personal injury/tort concept. So file within a year. 

- **Tutors, coaches, lessons not provided:** If you paid in advance for lessons or sessions (e.g. academic tutoring, music lessons, sports coaching, personal training) and the other party didn’t deliver them (or only partially did), you can sue for the unused portion of your money. *Examples:* You paid a piano teacher $400 for 10 lessons, but they only gave 4 lessons and then quit – you’d sue for the value of the 6 undelivered lessons (e.g. $240 if pro-rated). Or you hired a SAT tutor for a package of 20 hours for $1,000, and after 5 hours they stopped showing up – you could claim the remaining $750. These arrangements are often informal (maybe no written contract beyond text or email agreements). Still, your proof of payment and an outline of what was expected (10 lessons, etc.) will be your evidence. **Evidence:** Proof of payment (canceled check, Venmo record, etc.) and any correspondence detailing the agreement (“I’ll tutor your child 2 hours a week for 5 weeks”) and the breach (“Sorry, I can’t continue,” or records of missed sessions). If you didn’t have an explicit per-session rate, the court will just divide the total by number of sessions to figure out the refund. *Quirk:* If the person provided some benefit (those few lessons), you can’t get money back for those – only for what you didn’t get. If the quality was poor, that’s harder – you generally can’t not pay for lessons you did receive unless they were truly worthless. These cases are usually about **non-performance** rather than quality. Prescriptive period: It’s a breach of contract – 10 years if written or oral. But practically, you’ll probably file quickly once it’s clear they aren’t honoring the deal. Good faith: If the tutor partially performed and had to stop due to illness or something, a judge might be sympathetic but still will award your unused portion unless the contract said “no refunds.” (Even then, if they voluntarily quit, “no refunds” might not hold – generally that’s for if *you* cancel). This is a straightforward claim in small claims.

- **Pet services (boarding, grooming, vet) disputes:** If you paid for pet-related services and something went wrong – e.g. a pet groomer injured your pet, a boarding kennel lost or harmed your pet, or a dog trainer didn’t deliver on promised results, you may have a claim. *Examples:* A groomer cut your dog’s ear badly, incurring $300 in vet bills – you can sue the grooming salon for that $300 and maybe a refund of the grooming fee. Or you paid a pet boarding facility $200 to board your cat for a week, and due to negligence the cat got sick or escaped and was lost – you could sue for the value of your cat or vet bills, etc. Louisiana treats pets as property (as mentioned), so damage or loss to a pet is basically property damage. Emotional distress over an injured or lost pet typically isn’t compensated in dollars (courts have been reluctant to award that). But you can get the **economic losses**: vet bills, the replacement value of the pet (or value to you, which is tricky but for a trained animal could be substantial, though still subject to the $5k cap). For a service that simply wasn’t done (like a pet trainer who took your money and didn’t train), that’s a contract issue – you’d get unused portion back, similar to tutor scenarios. If a **veterinarian** was negligent and harmed your pet, that is essentially veterinary malpractice – Louisiana doesn’t have a special vet malpractice act, so it’s just negligence, and small claims can handle the damage to the pet (again, vet bills, etc.). If those bills exceed $5k (they easily can for serious cases), then you’d need to go to a higher court or waive some amount. *Pre-suit:* If something happened to your pet under someone’s care, definitely bring it up to them first – many kennels or groomers have insurance and might directly offer to cover vet bills. They might also ask you to sign a release if they pay – read it carefully. If they offer a reasonable settlement (e.g. they’ll pay your vet bill), it might be better to accept rather than litigate for possibly not much more (since emotional value isn’t usually awarded). If they deny responsibility, then small claims is appropriate. **Evidence:** All **vet medical records and bills** related to the incident, photos of your pet’s injury, any written incident report from the service provider, and any contract you signed with them. Note: many pet boarding or grooming contracts have liability waivers (except for gross negligence). Those waivers aren’t ironclad if the provider truly messed up, but they can make the case harder. Judges in small claims will still compensate out-of-pocket costs for clear negligence regardless of a generic waiver (they won’t enforce a waiver for, say, leaving a gate open and the pet escaping – that’s gross negligence normally). Prescriptive period: **1 year** from the incident (treat it as property damage or professional negligence). So do not delay on pet injury claims. Also consider – if it was extreme negligence or intentional harm to your pet, you could also file a complaint with the Louisiana Board of Veterinary Medicine (for vets) or possibly sue for mental anguish (rare, you’d need to show the incident caused you verifiable emotional distress beyond normal grief – seldom awarded). But small claims will mostly stick to economic loss.

- **Professional services (medical, legal, etc.):** If you feel a licensed professional (doctor, lawyer, accountant) did a poor job, usually those are “malpractice” claims that are beyond small claims – they often exceed $5k and require expert testimony. Louisiana specifically requires that **medical malpractice** claims go through a review panel if the claim is above $50,000 – effectively, you can’t bring even a $5k med mal claim directly without going through that process (and $5k med mal would be unusual anyway, given costs). Legal malpractice similarly often involves complex issues and higher stakes. So, while theoretically you could sue a professional in small claims for a small amount of damages if, say, they simply failed to complete a task (for instance, you paid a lawyer $2,000 and they did nothing – you might sue for a refund under a simple contract theory), be cautious: many of these are handled by specialty courts or arbitration. If it’s straightforward (like the lawyer clearly didn’t do the work, or the accountant you hired for $1,000 never filed your taxes as promised), it’s more of a breach of contract – small claims can handle a refund scenario. But if it’s about *quality* of professional work (like “my lawyer messed up my case and I think it’s worth $5,000”), that delves into malpractice and wouldn’t be suitable for small claims. In such cases, consult the professional’s regulatory board or a malpractice attorney. For the small set of instances where a professional simply didn’t deliver the agreed service and the damages are within $5k, you’d proceed like any other contract: show you paid, show they failed to do it, and demand your money back. For example, an attorney took your $1,000 retainer and then withdrew without doing anything substantive – you could seek the unused retainer back. The State Bar might also assist in fee disputes through arbitration. Keep in mind any **arbitration clauses** in engagement contracts (lawyers often don’t have those, but other pros might). The prescriptive period for breach of contract is 10 years, for professional malpractice it’s 1 year from discovery. But because small claims cannot grant the kind of relief malpractice cases usually seek, it’s generally not the forum for them except in the simple refund scenarios described.

### Loans, debts, and financial disputes

- **Money lent to friends or family:** This is one of the most common small claims nationwide – you lent someone money in good faith and they didn’t pay you back. We covered this under contract disputes, but to reiterate: if you **loaned personal funds** (with or without a written IOU), you can sue for repayment. In Louisiana, an oral loan is enforceable, though if over $500, Louisiana law (Civil Code art. 1846) says you need some kind of corroborating evidence of it (a witness or writing). So if you lent a friend $1,000 cash with no note, try to at least get a text from them like “I will pay you back.” That will satisfy the requirement. If all you have is “I gave them money and they promised to return it,” and they deny it, it becomes your word against theirs – the judge will evaluate credibility, but corroboration helps a lot in Louisiana due to that rule. As mentioned, you have up to 3 years or 10 years depending on circumstances (since “money lent” is specifically one of the obligations with a 3-year prescriptive period under Civ Code 3494)[28]. Best practice: file within 3 years of the loan (or of the date it was supposed to be repaid, if that was later). If the person made a **partial payment or wrote an email acknowledging the debt**, that can reset the time. Also, interest: if you didn’t explicitly agree on interest, you can’t randomly add it – you’d get legal interest from date of demand/judgment. If you did agree on interest, you can claim whatever interest had accrued (but keep the total within $5k for small claims). Small claims judges see a lot of these “friend/family loan” cases and may often ask, “Did you try to work it out? What was the money for? Why do you think they haven’t paid?” Be ready with a clear, non-emotional story and evidence. Don’t be embarrassed – these things happen, and the court is there to apply the agreement that was made.

- **Roommate and shared expense contributions:** If you and someone else agreed to split certain expenses and they failed to pay their share, you can sue for contribution. *Examples:* You fronted a $600 utility deposit for an apartment shared with a roommate on the understanding you’d split it, but the roommate never paid you their $300 half. Or you and a friend went on vacation and you covered the hotel costs of $800 with the promise they’d pay you back half, but they haven’t ($400). These are essentially informal contracts among peers. They are enforceable – you’d argue there was a **tacit agreement** to share the expense. **Evidence:** Ideally, a text or email where the person acknowledges “I’ll pay you back” or where you PayPal requested it and they didn’t object. If nothing in writing, describe the context (small claims judges deal with fairness in these scenarios – often it boils down to credibility if no proof). It helps if you can show a pattern (e.g., you each agreed to pay certain bills and you can show you paid multiple times and they didn’t). Louisiana doesn’t have a statute for “roommate agreements,” but general obligations law covers it. If you win, the judge might award exactly the unpaid share. If the arrangement wasn’t explicitly decided beforehand (e.g., you just hoped they’d chip in but never discussed it), that’s harder – an agreement needs meeting of the minds. But often, it’s implied by conduct (everyone expected to split). These claims are seen as **debt claims** – prescription could be 10 years (written or clearly implied contract). But don’t wait that long; file while memory is fresh (and before the person potentially leaves town!). 

- **Canceled engagements – ring and gifts:** If an engagement is called off, there can be dispute over the **engagement ring** and possibly expensive gifts. In Louisiana, an engagement ring is generally considered a **conditioned gift** – conditioned on the marriage taking place. Louisiana courts have followed a rule that if the engagement is broken, the ring should be returned to the giver, *especially* if the giver (usually man in older cases) was without fault in the breakup. (If the giver unjustifiably broke it off, courts might let the recipient keep the ring as damages – but that’s not settled like statute, it’s more equitable.) In small claims, you can sue for the return of the ring or its value (if the other person sold it or refuses to give it back). Often, just the threat of a suit will motivate its return. *Example:* You gave a $3,000 engagement ring and your fiancée broke off the engagement – you should get the ring back; if not, you can sue for the ring (or its value, up to $3k). Judges commonly follow that approach as it’s logical – the purpose of the gift (marriage) failed. **Note:** You cannot in small claims *force* someone to perform (e.g., marry you or apologize) – only money or property. So for the ring, you’d actually be seeking the item’s return. Small claims judges *can* order the transfer of property as equitable relief (they have authority to do appropriate relief)[2]. They might say “return the ring or pay $X value.” So, describe the ring (carat, etc.) and have evidence of its value (receipt or appraisal). As for other gifts between engaged couples: Louisiana doesn’t have a specific law – some states say conditioned on marriage, others say gifts in contemplation of marriage should be returned. Likely, expensive gifts specific to the marriage (like heirloom family jewelry, or contributions to a down payment on a house you were going to share) might be argued as conditional as well. In small claims, you could attempt to recover such contributions under unjust enrichment or implied contract theories. The outcome can be uncertain – courts generally focus on the ring. Prescriptive period: probably **1 year** from breakup for ring recovery (since it’s arguably a revocation of gift – maybe treated as quasi-contract). So don’t delay. Also consider emotional angles – these cases involve heartbreak; try to stick to facts and property. If you’re on the receiving end and believe you shouldn’t have to return it (perhaps because the giver was at fault), that could be your defense. But fault is murky to prove. Many people just return the ring to avoid court. Small claims can handle it if necessary.

- **Property division after breakups (unmarried couples):** If you and a former partner jointly purchased items or have commingled property and can’t agree how to split it, small claims can only deal with the **money value** – it cannot adjudicate title to real estate or enforce complex divisions. But, for example, if you and an ex each contributed to buying a $5,000 boat and agreed (even implicitly) to share or co-own it, and now one keeps the boat, the other could sue for their contribution or the boat’s value share (e.g. half). Essentially you’d claim an **interest in property** that can be resolved by a payment. *Example:* You and your ex bought furniture together, total $2,000, each paid $1,000. After breaking up, the ex kept all the furniture – you could sue for $1,000 (your half). Judges often handle this as an equitable monetary settlement. It’s tricky because technically if property is jointly titled, the proper remedy is a partition action in district court. But for small personal property, in practice, small claims courts do entertain these disputes by just valuing and splitting. If the items are easily divisible, a judge might even just facilitate who keeps what – but since they only have monetary jurisdiction, they usually convert it to dollars (“defendant can keep the furniture but must pay plaintiff $X for their share”). If real estate or a car with both names is at stake, *small claims cannot remove one’s name or change title* – that’s beyond its scope (and likely above $5k anyway). You’d have to either mutually sell and split proceeds or go to district court for partition. But for moderate-value personal property, small claims works on principles of **unjust enrichment** or **tacit partnership**. Bring proof of what you paid (receipts, bank statements). If there was an explicit agreement on splitting costs or ownership, mention that. If not explicit, the court often assumes equal shares if both contributed similarly. Prescriptive period: possibly **10 years** since it could be considered co-ownership claims (which prescribe in 30 years for property partition, or not at all until a demand is made). But once separated, don’t sit on it – file within a reasonable time (a year or two) for fairness. 

- **Security deposits and shared housing expenses:** (Security deposits are covered extensively in Landlord–Tenant below, but note here as well: if you and roommates each paid part of an apartment security deposit and the landlord unjustifiably kept it, you might team up to sue the landlord – or one of you might sue and represent others’ interests with their written consent, though technically each might need to be a plaintiff. Up to 10 can join in one suit, remember. Small claims could handle, say, three roommates jointly suing for a $1,500 deposit split among them.) For other shared expenses like utilities, cleaning fees on move-out, etc., similar logic as above – if one person fronted it on behalf of all, they can claim each’s share.

- **Creditor vs debtor suits:** Often, companies or lenders will sue debtors in regular city court, not small claims, especially if using attorneys. However, occasionally a **credit union, bank, or collection agency** might file in small claims for smaller consumer debts (under $5k) because it’s cheaper and faster (and attorneys *can* participate in Louisiana small claims, unlike some states). There is *no* prohibition in Louisiana against a **assignee or collection agency** using small claims – the law limiting assignees (like California has) does not exist here. So if you, as a creditor or collector, are owed say $4,000 on a defaulted loan or service contract, you can file in small claims. Likewise, a **landlord** might choose small claims to get a judgment for rent owed (staying under $5k). One limitation: if you are a **business operating under a trade name (d/b/a)**, you must be properly registered with the parish or state, or your suit could be dismissed until you register. Also, if you’re an out-of-state business not registered in Louisiana, the court might not let you maintain the action until you do register (Louisiana law requires a foreign entity doing business to be in good standing to use state courts). As a debtor being sued, know that small claims is an *informal* setting but a judgment there is as valid as any other. If you owe it and can pay, better to settle; if you dispute it, come with evidence (receipts, correspondence) and remember any defenses (e.g., prescription – many consumer debts in Louisiana have a **3-year** prescription if open account or **5-year** if a promissory note). You can raise that and if it’s prescribed (time-barred), the judge should dismiss it. Also, a debtor could **remove to regular court** to preserve appeal, but that often just drags out the inevitable unless you have a counterclaim or need more formal process. 

### Vehicle-specific disputes

*(Some of these overlap with earlier categories, but here we focus on disputes unique to vehicles.)*

- **Insurance denials for car damage:** If you had an accident and the other driver’s insurance won’t pay (perhaps they dispute fault or say damages are below deductible), you can sue the **other driver** in small claims for your vehicle damage (as described under vehicle accidents). You generally cannot directly sue the *insurance company* in small claims unless you also name their insured – but thanks to Louisiana’s Direct Action law, you actually *can* name the insurer as a co-defendant if the policy and accident fall under Louisiana law ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77967#:~:text=arbitration)). This is a bit technical, but bottom line: you can include the insurance company in your small claim (and serve them through the Louisiana Secretary of State). The insurer might then step in and defend. Often, just suing the driver (and letting them forward it to their insurer) is enough. If your **own** insurance unfairly denied your claim (e.g. your comprehensive coverage refused to pay for theft saying it’s not covered but you think it is), you technically could sue your insurer in small claims for the coverage amount. However, many insurance policies have an arbitration clause or require disputes to go to district court – and if it’s a major insurer, expect them to **remove to a higher court** or move to compel arbitration if you try small claims. Also, disputes involving interpretation of policy language can be complex. If it’s a clear-cut small amount (say, they didn’t reimburse a towing charge of $150 that clearly was covered), you might file a small claim as a tactic – they might just pay rather than litigate. But be aware, suing your own insurer triggers rules under Louisiana’s bad-faith statutes, etc., which exceed small claims scope. Most such issues are handled via Department of Insurance complaint or negotiations. 

- **Mechanic’s shop disputes:** Already covered in consumer disputes. Use small claims to recoup money from poor auto repairs, as described above.

- **Dealer sales disputes:** Covered above (refund for defective used car, undisclosed defects, etc.). Small claims can address modest claims like forcing a dealer to reimburse you the cost of needed repairs they failed to disclose, as long as within $5k. Note: If a **car dealer** sold you a clunker and possibly violated the Louisiana Used Motor Vehicle Commission’s rules or committed fraud, you can both sue and report them to the Commission. But serious fraud cases often exceed $5k (and might involve multiple victims – class action or AG involvement possibly). Keep small claims for the simpler scenario: e.g. dealer agreed in writing to fix an issue as part of sale but never did – you can sue for the cost you incurred to fix it, up to $5k.

- **Towing and impound fees:** If your car was **wrongfully towed or booted** and you had to pay fees, or a towing company damaged your car, you can sue for a refund or damages. *Examples:* Your vehicle was towed from your apartment despite having a proper permit, and you paid $300 in tow and storage charges – you can sue the towing company (and property owner perhaps) to get that $300 back. Or a tow truck driver caused $800 damage dragging your car – sue for the repair cost. Louisiana heavily regulates non-consensual towing fees (max rates set by the Public Service Commission) and requires tow companies to follow certain steps (like signage on parking lots, immediate notice to police, etc.). If those rules were violated, that strengthens your case that the tow was illegal. **Evidence:** Show you were legally parked or that procedures weren’t followed (photos of lack of signage, receipts showing you were a customer at the business from which you were towed, etc.). If your car was damaged, get a repair estimate and photos of the damage (and ideally a before-photo to show it was not damaged prior). There’s also a New Orleans ordinance sometimes invoked for towing issues if in Orleans Parish. *Pre-suit:* Often, disputes over towing/booting can be addressed by contacting the property owner or towing company – some have claim forms for damages. But many will stonewall, counting on people not knowing their rights. Small claims is an effective remedy here, because the towing company or booting outfit might rather refund a few hundred than send an employee to court and risk the court cutting into their practices. If you paid under protest, mention that (e.g., note on receipt “paid under protest” if you did so). Prescriptive period on a tort (damage to car) is 1 year, on an unlawful fee it’s arguably 1 year (as a quasi-offense or as a suit for recovery of a payment not owed). So assume **1 year**. Note: If your car was **wrongly repossessed** by a lender (no default or they breached peace), that’s a different scenario – often subject to conversion claims and maybe a wrongful repossession law. Those damages can be more than $5k (including maybe emotional distress for an illegal repo). Those are usually handled in larger courts. But if it’s a small-scale error (say you had paid off the loan but the bank accidentally authorized a repo, and you had to pay $200 to get your car back before they realized the mistake), you could use small claims for that out-of-pocket loss.

- **Parking lot damage (hit-and-run if known):** If your parked car was hit by someone and you identified the culprit (maybe through surveillance or witness) and they refuse to pay, you can sue them in small claims for the damage. This is basically like any car accident claim – we covered that. Often hit-and-run offenders, once identified, will be reported to their insurance (or charged criminally). But civilly, you have one year to act. Provide any evidence tying them to the damage (witness statements, matching paint, etc.). It may devolve into he-said/she-said if they deny it – having a police report that concludes they likely did it is very helpful. The court can order them to pay for repairs just like any accident. 

### Personal injury (small / minor cases)

Small claims courts are generally not the venue for significant **personal injury** or illness claims because the $5,000 cap is usually far below the value of these cases (medical bills alone often exceed $5k). However, for **minor injuries or incidents where damages are modest**, you can use small claims. *Examples:* You slipped on a store’s wet floor, had a $1,200 ER bill and a few days of soreness – you might be comfortable seeking, say, $2,000 total (medical + a bit for inconvenience) in small claims rather than pursuing a larger lawsuit. Or a neighbor’s dog nipped you causing a shallow wound and a $300 urgent care visit – that’s viable for small claims. Another example: a minor fender-bender where you weren’t really hurt badly but want to recover the precautionary medical check costs (say $500) and maybe $500 for pain – small claims can handle that $1,000 ask.

**Be realistic with damages:** If you had any substantial injury (broken bone, lengthy therapy, etc.), those cases usually exceed $5k (and might involve future complications), so they belong in regular court often with an attorney. But for **minor injuries** (soft tissue pain for a week, small laceration, etc.), you might prefer the speed of small claims to at least get your out-of-pocket expenses recouped. Louisiana does not restrict personal injury from small claims by statute – it’s allowed as long as you’re okay with the $5k limit and no jury.

**Evidence:** Bring any **medical records or bills** for treatment you received. In small claims, you typically won’t have a doctor testify, but you can supply the clinic report and bill – judges will accept that as proof of medical expense (they may not give weight to any written “diagnosis” beyond what’s needed to understand the treatment, but they will see you sought care and what it cost). If you missed work and lost wages (and the amount is small), bring a note from employer or pay stubs to quantify that. For pain and suffering, you won’t have concrete evidence beyond your testimony – describe what you went through (e.g., “I couldn’t lift heavy items for two weeks and endured a lot of pain in my shoulder, couldn’t sleep well, etc.”). Don’t exaggerate – small claims judges see a lot of people who embellish; be honest and straightforward. They might award a few hundred dollars for pain on top of medical costs if it seems warranted.

**Liability proof:** Just like any injury case, you need to show the other party was at fault. If it was a premises liability (like a store slip), you should show they were negligent (maybe pictures of the spill, lack of warning signs, etc.). If it’s a dog bite, prove it happened and that you didn’t provoke the dog. If it’s a minor auto accident, prove the other driver was in the wrong (police report, etc.). In small claims, the formal rules of evidence are relaxed, but the legal standards (like comparative fault) still apply. 

**No attorneys (likely):** In small claims, you’ll present the injury case yourself. The defendant (or their insurance if involved) might send an attorney – note that in Louisiana small claims, attorneys *can* participate (unlike some states). If the defendant’s insurance company decides to defend, they may even try to remove the case to a regular court (especially if they suspect you’re aiming for just under cap but actually the claim might be worth more). However, if your claimed damages are clearly limited to < $5k, they might just stay in small claims. Often, insurance companies settle small claims suits promptly when liability is clear, to avoid the hassle and added attorney fees on their side. So filing can prompt a last-minute settlement offer around the time of trial.

**Prescriptive period:** Most personal injury claims in Louisiana prescribe (expire) **1 year** from the date of injury (or from when you discovered the injury, if it wasn’t immediately evident). This includes slips, dog bites, minor car injuries, etc. So you must file within one year. There is no “tolling” in small claims just because you’re negotiating – one year is strict for torts.

**Limitations:** Small claims judges generally will not award **punitive damages** or huge pain & suffering amounts. Louisiana largely prohibits punitive damages except in rare statutory cases (e.g. drunk driving injuries can get punitive damages, but those cases aren’t suited for small claims as damages are typically high and require district court). So in small claims, you’re looking at **actual damages** (medical, lost wages, property damage) and some **general damages** for pain and suffering if appropriate – but kept modest to fit the cap. Also, note that if your injury was caused by a **common carrier or the state or a medical provider**, special rules or immunities might apply that small claims judges may not want to wade into. For instance, if you’re suing a state agency for an injury on state property, they might have immunity or you might need to go to district court (plus, the state can insist on trial in district court). For typical private defendant cases, small claims is fine.

**Example resolution:** Suppose you slipped in a grocery store, no serious injury but $1,000 in bills and inconvenience. You could sue for, say, $2,500. The grocery store’s insurance might offer you $1,500 before trial. You weigh the risk – in small claims the judge might split the difference or ask, “Did you not see the spill? Maybe you’re 20% at fault.” They might then award around $2,000 minus 20% = $1,600. Considering that, $1,500 to settle isn’t far off. Small claims outcomes can be a bit of a Solomon’s justice sometimes. But you save time and attorney fees doing it this route.

In summary, **yes**, you can handle small personal injury cases in small claims, but be mindful of the value and proof. If it involves even moderate complexity or value, it may be better in a higher court or through an attorney. Small claims is best for **minor injuries where damages are clearly under $5,000** and you just want to be made whole for your medical expenses and a bit of discomfort.

### Specific statutory claims

Louisiana has a number of statutes that allow additional damages or specific remedies which can be pursued in small claims as long as the total stays within $5k. We’ve touched on many in context above, but here’s a recap of key ones:

- **Bad check penalties:** As noted, Louisiana’s law for NSF checks (R.S. 9:2782) lets you recover **2× the check amount (minimum $100)** plus attorney fees and costs if proper demand was made . This can be pursued in small claims (cap applies). Always follow the certified demand letter procedure to be eligible.

- **Final wage penalties:** Under R.S. 23:632, an employer who fails to pay final wages can owe **90 days of wage penalty** plus attorney fees ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=This%20media,not%20necessarily%20the%20official%20citation)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/revised-statutes/title-13/rs-13-5203/#:~:text=of%20substantive%20law%2C%20and%20shall,provisions%20relating%20to%20privileged%20communications)). This can be awarded in small claims (subject to the $5k cap). In practice, judges often award some penalty if the facts merit it, though sometimes less than the full 90 days depending on the circumstance (good-faith dispute, etc.). But by law, up to 90 days can be given – just be aware if 90 days wages exceeds $5k, you’d cap your ask or go to a different court. Small claims judges do have authority to award these statutory penalties since they are “provided by law” (the $5k limit explicitly excludes “penalties provided by law” from the calculation, meaning you could arguably get wages + penalty even if that sum > $5k, but since the statute excludes penalties from jurisdictional limit calculation, there’s an argument the $5k cap is on the base wages only[1]. But to be safe, treat $5k as total cap – don’t expect a small claims judge to go beyond it). 

- **Unfair trade practices (LUTPA) damages:** Louisiana’s Unfair Trade Practices Act allows **actual damages**, and if the conduct was notified by AG as egregious, **treble damages**, plus mandatory attorney fees for a prevailing consumer ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77969#:~:text=Committees%20,Input)). In small claims, realistically you’re seeking the actual damages and maybe attorney fees. Treble damages would often overshoot $5k or require proving the AG notice (rare). But you should definitely request attorney fees if you win a LUTPA claim (and you had any attorney cost or even just as a statutory right – some judges might award a few hundred for your self-representation efforts, but that’s discretionary). Example: You sue a contractor for taking $4,000 and doing nothing (deceptive practice). The judge finds it was intentional and egregious – under LUTPA, he must award you $4,000 plus attorney fees. If you had a lawyer behind the scenes or paid for consult, you could evidence that cost. If not, you might at least get full costs. The **prescription** on LUTPA is one year, so act quickly.

- **Lemon Law:** Louisiana’s new car Lemon Law (R.S. 51:1941-1950) provides that if a new vehicle has serious defects not fixed after a reasonable attempts (usually 4 attempts or 30 days out of service in first year), the owner is entitled to a replacement or refund. Usually these claims exceed $5k (car value), and manufacturers often have arbitration programs. You *could* use small claims for a **diminished value or repair cost** claim under lemon law if, say, the car is drivable but with defects and you’re willing to accept under $5k to compensate. However, typically one would pursue the full remedy via the designated arbitration or larger court. Small claims might come into play for a *used car* under an implied warranty (redhibition) which we covered, or maybe for incidental costs like towing or rental that the manufacturer refuses to cover – but those are usually handled amicably if a lemon repurchase is happening. In short, Lemon Law per se – not usually a small claim, except possibly as part of a bigger negotiation or a nuisance suit to get attention.

- **Identity theft and fraud victims:** Louisiana law (R.S. 9:3568) allows identity theft victims to recover some costs (like the cost of restoring credit) from the thief. In practice, suing the person who stole your identity is rare (they’re often unknown or judgment-proof). But if, say, someone you know fraudulently opened a credit line in your name and you cleared it but had out-of-pocket expenses (fees, maybe the account you paid to avoid credit damage), you could sue that person for those losses. Also, Louisiana has a general fraud statute (Civil Code art. 1953) – if someone defrauded you in a transaction, you can rescind the contract or get damages. That often overlaps with LUTPA or contract claims. Small claims can handle straightforward fraud if under $5k – e.g., you paid $1,000 for something that turned out to be a scam, you can sue to get it back. No special damages beyond actual loss are given for ordinary fraud (except maybe you could get attorney fees under LUTPA if applicable). The prescriptive period for fraud is one year from discovery.

- **Trebled damages for certain offenses:** Some Louisiana statutes provide for multiplied damages in civil suits. Example: If someone writes you a **bad check** as described, that’s double (we covered that). If someone **steals timber** off your land, Louisiana law (R.S. 3:4278.1) says you can get treble the value of the timber. If that happened on a small scale (say $1,000 of trees, trebled to $3,000), you could use small claims. Or if a landlord **locks out a tenant** illegally, the tenant can get a statutory penalty (could be two months’ rent plus $200 per R.S. 9:3260 – but that often exceeds $5k if rent high). For *common small scenarios* though, the main treble/double provisions we see are the bad checks and maybe consumer fraud under LUTPA (treble with AG notice). There is also the federal Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) that allows $500 per unwanted call/text ($1,500 if willful). Some consumers have sued telemarketers in small claims for those statutory amounts – that is allowed since TCPA gives state courts jurisdiction and the amounts are often small. If you do that, cite TCPA and proof of the calls – some people have indeed gotten a few thousand via small claims from spam callers (assuming you can serve the company). This is niche but worth noting.

- **Wage penalty (waiting time) & bad check – summary:** these are probably the most commonly invoked statutory extras in small claims, and Louisiana’s are quite favorable to plaintiffs (double checks, 90-days wages). Judges will enforce them if the criteria are met. Always cite the statute in your claim form or at least at trial and provide proof you did the required steps (e.g. demand letters).

In general, if a Louisiana statute provides a specific damage or penalty and your case meets the criteria, **small claims court can award it.** Just be mindful of the $5,000 cap – if the statutory damages blow past $5k, you might have to abandon the excess or file in a higher court. The small claims statute explicitly excludes interest, costs, attorney fees, and statutory penalties from the jurisdictional amount calculation ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/Legis/Law.aspx?d=77961#:~:text=A,the%20Code%20of%20Civil%20Procedure)), meaning you don’t count those toward the $5k when determining if you’re under the limit. For example, a $4,000 principal claim with $3,000 in statutory penalty could theoretically be heard (principal + penalty = $7k > $5k, but since the penalty isn’t counted, the jurisdictional amount is $4k, which is allowed). In practice, however, some small claims judges might be uncomfortable awarding a huge penalty that goes beyond $5k total. There’s an argument legally they can, but be aware of this nuance. If your case is like that (e.g., 90 days of wages = $9k penalty on a $500 wage claim), you might be better in a regular court, or explicitly waive a portion of the penalty to stay within $5k. 

### Other state-specific claim types

*(This section is an open-ended catch-all for any niche or uncommon state-specific claims that might arise in small claims. We’ll list a few that haven’t been covered yet, to ensure comprehensiveness.)*

- **Towing without authorization (vehicle booting/towing laws):** We mentioned this under vehicle disputes. Louisiana law (and local ordinances) provide rights to vehicle owners regarding towing. For instance, New Orleans has specific rules and penalties for illegal booting/towing. If your car was towed in violation of those rules, you can claim any statutory damages or fee refunds provided. Another example: If a **predatory towing** company overcharged beyond the allowed fee schedule, you can sue to recover the illegal portion. Know the precise law or ordinance – e.g., “City ordinance says max boot fee $90, they charged me $150, I seek the $60 difference back.” Provide proof of what you paid and the ordinance text (judges might not know it offhand).

- **Consumer lease disputes:** If you leased something (a car, furniture) and the lessor violated the Louisiana Lease of Personal Property Act, you might have a claim. E.g., some rent-to-own contracts have protections. If it’s a small-dollar issue (under $5k), small claims can handle, say, a wrongful repossession of leased furniture resulting in loss of your equity. 

- **HOA and condo fines:** If your homeowner association hit you with an improper fine under $5k and you refuse to pay, they might not go to the expense of formal court – you could affirmatively sue in small claims to **challenge the fine and get a declaratory judgment not exactly** (small claims can’t issue declarations), but you could effectively ask for refund if you paid, or if you haven’t paid, you typically wait to be sued. HOAs often have internal dispute resolution. But say you *did* pay a $2000 fine under protest and later realized it was not authorized, you could try small claims to recover it. A judge will look at HOA bylaws, etc. There’s no guarantee – some judges might say it’s beyond small claims scope if it involves interpreting covenants. But financially it’s a straightforward claim of money had and received. Must weigh the complexity – many HOA matters might better go to ordinary civil court. 

- **Mold and landlord health/safety statute claims:** Louisiana’s statutes regarding landlord duties (like providing safe habitable housing) allow tenants to fix issues and deduct or claim damages in some cases (Civil Code arts. 2694, 2696). For instance, if a landlord fails to repair an issue affecting health and you spend money to remedy it (like you paid $300 for mold remediation after landlord neglected a leak), you can claim that expense. Or if the law provides a penalty for landlord’s bad-faith retention of property (some states have triple rent damages for unlawful eviction – Louisiana allows recovery of actual damages and possibly penalties for unlawful eviction/utility cutoff under certain laws). These again cross into landlord-tenant, but mention here to ensure completeness: small claims can address those financial remedies. A specific example: If a landlord **unlawfully keeps a tenant’s personal property** after eviction (in Louisiana, R.S. 9:3257 provides a process for tenants to retrieve property), a tenant might sue for the value of items not returned. Or if a **self-storage facility** wrongfully sold your stored items, you can sue for the loss (though storage contracts often limit liability heavily). These are just further applications of conversion/negligence law.

- **Minor credit reporting or debt collection violations:** Louisiana has its own **Consumer Credit Law** and a version of the Fair Debt Collection Practices Act in R.S. 9:3562, which prohibits certain abusive collection tactics ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/2014/code-revisedstatutes/title-13/rs-13-5201/#:~:text=RS%2013%3A5201%20,in%20existence%20or%20hereafter%20created)) ([law.justia.com](https://law.justia.com/codes/louisiana/2014/code-revisedstatutes/title-13/rs-13-5201/#:~:text=RS%2013%3A5201%20,in%20existence%20or%20hereafter%20created)). If a debt collector harassed you and you have actual damages (say you paid a $200 counsel fee to send a cease letter, or you suffered a loss due to their improper disclosure of your debt to someone which cost you a job opportunity), you could theoretically sue for those damages under that state law or federal FDCPA (FDCPA gives up to $1,000 statutory damages for harassment plus actual damages and fees). Federal FDCPA claims can be filed in state court, and $1,000 is squarely within small claims. If you have clear evidence of violations (like recorded calls at odd hours, threats, etc.), you could use small claims to get the statutory $1,000. The collection agency may decide to pay or settle rather than fight in a local small court. Ensure you have documentation of the misconduct. Note: these cases sometimes allow attorney fees – if you don’t have one, you likely just get statuory amount. But some consumers do pursue them successfully. The prescriptive period for FDCPA is 1 year. State law for unauthorized collection actions doesn’t explicitly provide a private action (it’s often used as a defense or LUTPA claim). But you could bundle it as LUTPA (unfair practice by collector) and get fees.

- **Small contractor fraud (Home Improvement Fraud Act):** Louisiana has a criminal statute for home improvement fraud (contractor takes money and doesn’t perform). It doesn’t directly give a civil cause, but victims can sue for their money under general law. If the amount is small enough for small claims, that probably means the loss was < $5k. We’ve covered that scenario in contract disputes. Just noting there’s no special treble damages or fund to pay you – you just get actual losses. Sometimes if there’s a criminal case, the court may order restitution, which could overlap with your claim (you can’t double recover).

- **Agricultural disputes:** If a neighbor’s farm animal strays and eats your crops under a certain value, or fence disputes where small damages occur, these can be handled like property damage. Louisiana’s Stock Law might impose certain liabilities. But usually it’s straightforward negligence or fence law (Civil Code has some articles requiring neighbors to maintain fences when one has livestock). For a small claim – e.g., “my neighbor’s cow destroyed $500 of my garden” – small claims can award $500. 

- **Fishing/hunting expense disputes:** Not common, but say you paid a guide $600 for a guided fishing trip and they never took you out – you could get that back in small claims (service not delivered). Or you leased a hunting camp and it was not as promised, you might seek a partial refund if within $5k. These are just more contract cases.

The point of this section is: virtually any **state-law cause of action for money** under $5,000 can be brought in small claims **unless** it’s specifically excluded or requires a different forum. We have covered the major ones. If something isn’t explicitly barred (see the next section) and it resulted in you losing money or property under $5k, chances are you can sue for it here.

### Counterclaims

In Louisiana small claims court, a **defendant** who is sued can **file a counterclaim** (called a “reconventional demand” in Louisiana procedure) against the plaintiff if the defendant believes the plaintiff actually owes *them* money related to the same dispute. Here’s how counterclaims work:

- **Allowed amount:** A counterclaim in small claims is generally limited to the same jurisdictional maximum – **$5,000**. If the amount the defendant seeks is within $5k, it stays in small claims as a counterclaim. The case essentially becomes plaintiff’s claim vs. defendant’s counterclaim, both to be decided together. 

- **If counterclaim exceeds $5,000:** Louisiana law provides a mechanism for this. If a defendant believes they have a claim **above $5,000** against the plaintiff arising from the same matter, the defendant can **remove the case out of small claims** to a court of competent jurisdiction (the city court’s regular civil docket or to district court) before trial. The statute says if the counterclaim is over the small claims jurisdiction, the defendant may file an action in the appropriate court and file an affidavit in small claims, and then the small claim *shall* be transferred to that court and consolidated with the new action ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77967#:~:text=)) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77967#:~:text=%C2%A75207)). In practice, the defendant must usually act quickly (generally, **at or before the time of filing an answer** or at least before the small claims trial) to request transfer. The small claims judge will then send the case to the ordinary civil court for both claims to be heard there. This preserves the defendant’s ability to recover the full amount (and typically their right to a jury or appeal in the larger court).

- **Procedure to file counterclaim:** To counter-sue in small claims, the defendant should **file a written Answer** (which can often be done on a small claims answer form) and include in it the reconventional demand (counterclaim) detailing how much is claimed and why. Many small claims courts have a simple **“Defendant’s Answer and Counterclaim”** form. (For example, Baton Rouge City Court provides a “Defendant’s Answer (PDF)” form where a section for counterclaim can be filled in ([www.brla.gov](https://www.brla.gov/319/Civil-Suit-Interactive-Forms#:~:text=1,Request%20for%20Transfer%20%28PDF)).) You usually do this **before the trial date** – ideally within the 10-day answer period after service . However, even if the defendant doesn’t file in advance, some small claims judges will allow the counterclaim to be raised at trial – but officially, to enforce your rights, put it in writing beforehand so the plaintiff is aware. There is a small filing fee for a counterclaim in some courts (for instance, Baton Rouge charges $20 to file a reconventional demand in small claims). But it’s often negligible, and in some courts, the answer fee might cover it. Check with the clerk.

- **Same case vs new case:** If the counterclaim is within $5k, it remains as part of the same small claims case. Both claims will be heard together by the small claims judge. If the counterclaim is properly over $5k and the defendant wants to pursue the full amount, the defendant should file a **separate suit in regular court** for that claim and **move to transfer** the small claim. Louisiana law (R.S. 13:5206 and R.S. 13:5220 under a newer pilot program) lays out this process: The defendant files an affidavit in small claims stating the claim exceeds the jurisdiction and that they have filed an ordinary suit in a specified court ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77967#:~:text=%C2%A75207)) ([www.legis.la.gov](https://www.legis.la.gov/legis/Law.aspx?d=77967#:~:text=WHICH%20YOU%20RECEIVE%20THIS%20NOTICE,OTHER%20PARTIES%20IN%20THIS%20MATTER)). The small claims judge then transfers the case to that court, and the two actions are consolidated for trial in the higher court . The small claims plaintiff isn’t required to pay a new filing fee when it’s transferred; the law says the plaintiff will not have to pay transmittal or additional filing fees upon transfer , whereas the defendant does pay the standard fees to file their larger action (and a $10 transmittal fee in the small claims, per R.S. 13:5206(C)).

- **Strategic use:** This setup essentially means if a **defendant’s counterclaim is above $5k**, they have two options: (1) **waive the amount over $5,000** and sue within small claims for up to $5k (some courts may require an explicit waiver on record), or (2) remove to regular court to claim the full amount (losing the small claims speed/low-cost benefit, but preserving their full rights). If a counterclaim is, say, $6,000, a defendant might decide to just waive $1,000 and stay in small claims, since going to district court could cost more in time and attorney fees. However, if it’s much larger – definitely move it out. 

- **Hearing and judgment:** When a counterclaim is heard in small claims (i.e., it’s ≤ $5k or defendant waived the rest), the judge at trial will effectively consider **both** claims together. For example, a landlord sued a former tenant for $2,000 in damages, and the tenant counterclaimed for $1,500 of unreturned security deposit. The small claims judge can weigh both: maybe deciding the landlord’s claimed damages are partly valid, and the tenant’s deposit was indeed wrongly withheld in part. The judge might net them out or issue separate awards. In Louisiana, the court can issue a judgment that **offsets** the amounts (called compensation). If the defendant ends up proving more damages than the plaintiff, the judgment can actually be in the **defendant’s favor** for the difference. (Small claims division is authorized to grant any appropriate relief including money damages to either party[2].) Using our example, if the judge finds the tenant should get $1,000 of deposit and landlord had $500 in valid damages, the judgment might say “$500 to defendant (net)” or simply “plaintiff shall recover $0, and defendant is awarded $500 on the counterclaim.” Louisiana law explicitly says a plaintiff who chooses small claims **waives the right to appeal** the result (unless it’s removed)[7], so if a counterclaim turns the tables and the original plaintiff loses net, the plaintiff can’t appeal that outcome (nor can the defendant, since by not removing they waived appeal too). 

- **Counterclaim not related to plaintiff’s claim:** Generally, Louisiana requires that a reconventional demand arise out of the same transaction or occurrence as the main demand (or at least be connected). If a defendant tries to countersue the plaintiff for something entirely unrelated (e.g., plaintiff sues for a car accident, defendant counterclaims “well you owe me $500 from a completely separate loan”), the court might disallow it in that suit as not within scope. The defendant would have to file that as a separate small claim on their own. So counterclaims should be **related** to the original claim or at least between the same parties. (In Louisiana City Court practice, reconventional demands are allowed as in district court – CCP Art. 1061 – which requires some connexity if it exceeds certain jurisdiction. The small claims statutes don’t explicitly limit what the counterclaim can be, but standard practice does.)

- **Fees:** As noted, there’s typically a small fee for filing a counterclaim (like any lawsuit). R.S. 13:5206(C) mentions a $10 transmittal fee when transferring out, but in small claims itself, local rules often set a fee per defendant or per claim. Often it might just be the answer fee. Check your court’s fee schedule. The good news: if you win on a counterclaim, you can ask for court costs just like a plaintiff could (the prevailing party usually gets costs, so a successful counterclaimant can get their filing fee and service fee back as part of judgment).

- **No extra defendants via counterclaim:** Note that in small claims, the defendant cannot add new parties in a counterclaim (you can only counter-sue the existing plaintiff(s)). If your counterclaim somehow implicates a third party, that’s beyond small claims scope – you’d have to go to regular court to bring in third parties. Small claims is meant to be two sides: plaintiff vs. defendant. 

In summary, **defendants can assert their own claims** in small claims court in Louisiana. If it’s under $5k, it will be handled in the same hearing. If it’s more, the defendant should timely remove the case to preserve the larger claim (the law specifically provides for that transfer procedure to avoid prejudice to the defendant’s rights)[16][17]. This system prevents a situation where a plaintiff tries to use small claims to dodge a big counterclaim – if that scenario arises, the onus is on the defendant to escalate it to the proper court. And practically, if a defendant doesn’t remove and goes to trial in small claims, they’re considered to have **waived any claim over $5k** (they can’t later sue for the excess – the judgment in small claims would likely have res judicata effect on the whole dispute). So defendants, pay attention to that deadline (it’s “within the time to answer” by statute for removal as of right[7]). If you miss it, the judge still has discretion to transfer the case if a counterclaim over the limit pops up late, but don’t rely on that – act early.

### What you CANNOT sue for in small claims

While Louisiana small claims courts are versatile, there are certain types of cases and relief that **cannot** be pursued there. Below is a list of **excluded matters** – if your issue falls into one of these, small claims is not the proper forum:

- **Evictions and possession of real property:** You cannot use small claims to evict a tenant or otherwise regain possession of land or a rental unit. Eviction (called **“summary eviction”** or **“unlawful detainer”**) is handled by a separate section of City Court or Justice of the Peace Court as a **summary proceeding**, not through the small claims division. In fact, the small claims statute explicitly prohibits **summary proceedings** like evictions in small claims court . If you are a landlord seeking to evict, you must file an eviction proceeding (which often can still be in city court, but on its regular docket – many city courts have a dedicated eviction section). Likewise, a tenant cannot sue in small claims to obtain an order forcing a landlord to allow them back in or to stop an eviction – those are injunction/possessory matters, which small claims can’t grant.

- **Repossession of property / Specific performance:** Small claims is for money judgments. You generally *cannot* ask the small claims judge to order someone to *do something* (other than pay money or possibly return specific property like an item), nor can you ask the court to declare ownership or transfer title of property. For instance, you cannot sue in small claims to **force a seller to complete a sale** (specific performance) or to make an ex return personal items (though you *could* sue for the value of those items). You also cannot seek a court-ordered **injunction** in small claims – e.g., to make your neighbor stop building a fence on your land or to stop someone from harassing you. (Small claims cannot issue restraining orders – those go to family or district court.) The only slight exception is that small claims can order **equitable relief ancillary to a money judgment** – for example, as noted, a judge could order the return of an engagement ring or similar property as part of resolving a monetary dispute . But you wouldn’t sue solely for an injunction or declaratory judgment by itself.

- **Determining title to real estate:** If your case is about **who owns real property** or a boundary line, or any interest in real estate, small claims is not appropriate. Those issues (e.g., title disputes, **quiet title actions**, foreclosure of mortgages, etc.) are exclusively for district courts. Even if the dollar value is small or nominal, small claims can’t adjudicate property ownership. Also, small claims can’t handle **enforcing real estate contracts** (like specific performance of a purchase agreement) or **partition of real property** between co-owners. Those require formal proceedings in higher court.

- **Family law matters:** Small claims cannot be used for divorce, child custody, child support, spousal support, community property division, adoption, emancipation, or any other family law case. Those are handled by specialized family courts or district courts. Even if your claim is something like “my ex-husband promised to pay me $3,000 as part of our breakup and didn’t” – if it’s part of a marital settlement, it should be handled in the divorce case. (If it’s completely separate – e.g., after divorce, you loaned your ex $1,000 and they didn’t pay – that’s just a regular debt which you can do in small claims, not a family law issue.) But anything arising from marriage or needing a change to a divorce decree cannot go to small claims. Also, you can’t sue in small claims to modify or enforce a **child support order** – those go through family court’s enforcement mechanisms (or DCFS if applicable). 

- **Succession (probate) matters:** You cannot use small claims to resolve disputes over a deceased person’s estate (succession). For example, you can’t sue an estate in small claims for your inheritance or to interpret a will. Claims against a succession (like a debt the deceased owed you) must be filed in the succession proceeding in district court (or via a probate claim process). If the succession owes you a relatively small sum and is not properly paying, you still have to go through the ordinary probate court – small claims has no jurisdiction over succession funds or forcing an executor to pay. Similarly, you can’t adjudicate who are the heirs in small claims. 

- **Defamation, libel, slander:** Technically, you could try to sue for defamation in small claims if you only seek up to $5,000, but in practice, defamation cases typically involve non-economic harm (reputation, emotional distress) and can be complex (First Amendment issues, truth as a defense, etc.). Louisiana defamation suits often exceed $5k in claimed damages and belong in higher court. Small claims judges are likely uncomfortable deciding defamation – it’s not a simple debt or contract.
